Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c6} (spine r9c2â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r8c9, [r8c3=r9c6]}:
      Diagonals: {r5c6, r8c9} and {r5c9, [r8c3=r9c6]} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r8c9) = X
        col 6 {r5c6, r7c6, [r8c3=r9c6]}: r5c6=X â†’ r7c6, [r8c3=r9c6]â‰ X

        row 7 {r7c6, r7c7, r7c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r5c9) = color([r8c3=r9c6]) = X
        row 9 {[r8c3=r9c6], r9c2, r9c3}: [r8c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r8c3=r9c6], r9c3}: [r8c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X

      Each bridge forces opposite edges to miss different colors,
      but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r5c9, r6c9, r7c9} (spine r5c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c2, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c7}:

  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c8=r7c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r6c9, r6c3, r7c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  2.  Circular ladder {r8c2â€”r9c2, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c7}:
      Satellites r7c3 (rung 1), r5c6 (rung 2), [r6c8=r7c9] (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c8=r7c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r6c9, r6c3, r7c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r5c9, r6c9, r7c9} (spine r5c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c9, r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], r7c3} (spine r6c3â€”[r6c8=r7c9]).
      â†’ color(r6c9) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c9, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3], r6c3} (spine [r6c8=r7c9]â€”[r6c9=r7c3]).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  4.  Circular ladder {[r6c8=r7c9]â€”[r6c9=r7c3], r8c7â€”r8c2, r9c7â€”r9c1}:
      Satellites [r5c9=r6c3] (rung 1), r8c6 (rung 2), r9c6 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {r5c6, [r5c9=r6c3], r8c6, r9c6}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r5c9, r6c9, r7c9} (spine r5c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r8c2â€”r9c1, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c7â€”r9c7}:
      Satellites r7c3 (rung 1), r5c6 (rung 2), [r6c8=r7c9] (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c8=r7c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r6c9, r6c3, r7c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_guardian] (diamond+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r5c9, r6c9, r7c9} (spine r5c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c9, r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], r7c3} (spine r6c3â€”[r6c8=r7c9]).
      â†’ color(r6c9) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c2, r8c7, r9c7, r9c1}:
      Diagonals: {r8c2, r9c7} and {r8c7, r9c1} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r8c2) = color(r9c7) = X
        row 8 {r8c2, r8c6, r8c7}: r8c2=X â†’ r8c6, r8c7â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c1, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ r9c1, r9c6â‰ X

        box 9 {[r6c8=r7c9], r8c7, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ [r6c8=r7c9]â‰ X
        r5c6=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X

        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c7) = color(r9c1) = X
        row 8 {r8c2, r8c6, r8c7}: r8c7=X â†’ r8c2, r8c6â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c1, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c1=X â†’ r9c6, r9c7â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        box 7 {[r6c9=r7c3], r8c2, r9c1}: r9c1=X â†’ [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X
        box 9 {[r6c8=r7c9], r8c7, r9c7}: r8c7=X â†’ [r6c8=r7c9]â‰ X
        r5c6=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        col 9 {r5c9, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

      but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.

        row 6 {r6c3, r6c7, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c3} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c3). Identify.

      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r9c3). Identify.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r8c3, r9c6:
      Case A: color(r8c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r8c9, [r8c3=r9c6]}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r8c9} and {r5c9, [r8c3=r9c6]} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r8c9) = X
              col 6 {r5c6, r7c6, [r8c3=r9c6]}: r5c6=X â†’ r7c6, [r8c3=r9c6]â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c7, r7c8, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r7c7, r7c8â‰ X
              r5c6=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 7 {r7c6, r7c7, r7c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r5c9) = color([r8c3=r9c6]) = X
              row 9 {[r8c3=r9c6], r9c2, r9c3}: [r8c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, [r8c3=r9c6], r9c3}: [r8c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
              col 6 {r5c6, r7c6, [r8c3=r9c6]}: [r8c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r5c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8}: r5c9=X â†’ r6c7, r6c8â‰ X
              r5c9=X â†’ r8c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c7, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r8c3) â‰  color(r9c6). Add edge.
        3.  Kâ‚„ on {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c6}. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET+oddwheel) ===

      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c9). Identify.

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r8c3, r6c3, r9c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  4.  Diamond {[r5c9=r6c3=r9c2], r5c6, r9c6, r8c6} (spine r5c6â€”r9c6).

  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c9=r6c3=r8c6=r9c2], [r7c9=r8c3], r8c8, r9c7}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_ladder] (diamond+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c3} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c9, [r5c9=r6c3], r7c3, r8c3} (spine [r5c9=r6c3]â€”r7c3).
      â†’ color(r7c9) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, [r5c9=r6c3], [r7c9=r8c3], r8c6}:
      Diagonals: {r5c6, [r7c9=r8c3]} and {[r5c9=r6c3], r8c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c6) = color([r7c9=r8c3]) = X
        row 8 {[r7c9=r8c3], r8c6, r8c8}: [r7c9=r8c3]=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X
        col 3 {[r5c9=r6c3], r7c3, [r7c9=r8c3]}: [r7c9=r8c3]=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c3], r7c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r9c6â‰ X
        box 7 {r7c3, [r7c9=r8c3], r9c2}: [r7c9=r8c3]=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
        box 9 {[r7c9=r8c3], r8c8, r9c7}: [r7c9=r8c3]=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color([r5c9=r6c3]) = color(r8c6) = X
        row 6 {[r5c9=r6c3], r6c7, r6c8}: [r5c9=r6c3]=X â†’ r6c7, r6c8â‰ X
        row 8 {[r7c9=r8c3], r8c6, r8c8}: r8c6=X â†’ [r7c9=r8c3], r8c8â‰ X
        col 3 {[r5c9=r6c3], r7c3, [r7c9=r8c3]}: [r5c9=r6c3]=X â†’ r7c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6=X â†’ r5c6, r9c6â‰ X

        box 9 {[r7c9=r8c3], r8c8, r9c7}: [r7c9=r8c3], r8c8â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
        r9c2=X and r9c7=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              row 8 {r8c2, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c2, r8c6â‰ X

              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c6) â‰  color(r7c3). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r7c9, r7c3}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r7c9} and {r5c9, r7c3} (non-adjacent).

              Contradiction.

              row 6 {r6c3, r6c7, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c5â€”r9c5, r8c8â€”r9c8}:

      â†’ color(r6c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c9=r7c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r7c9, r6c3, r6c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+ladder) ===

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_parity] (ladder+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c5â€”r9c4, r8c9â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r7c6 (rung 2), r7c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r7c6, r8c5, r8c3}:
      Diagonals: {r6c3, r8c5} and {r7c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c3) = color(r8c5) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c8â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c9}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c3, r8c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r8c5=X â†’ r7c6, r9c4â‰ X
        r6c3=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c8}: r9c3, r9c4â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6, r7c6â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        col 9 {r5c9, r7c9, r8c9}: r7c9, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        r5c6=X and r5c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r7c6) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c9}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c5, r8c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r6c3, r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r5c6, r6c6â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r7c6=X â†’ r9c4â‰ X
        r7c6=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c3, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c8}: r9c3, r9c4â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        col 9 {r5c9, r7c9, r8c9}: r7c9, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        r5c9=X and r6c8=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_ladder] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c6, r6c8:
      Case A: color(r5c6) = color(r6c8). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c7, r9c7, r9c3}:
            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c7} and {r8c7, r9c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r8c3) = color(r9c7) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c7}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c5, r8c7â‰ X

              box 9 {r7c9, r8c7, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r7c6=X â†’ [r5c6=r6c8], r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r5c6=r6c8], r6c3, r6c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c7) = color(r9c3) = X

              row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c7}: r9c3=X â†’ r9c5, r9c7â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r9c3=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c5}: r8c5, r9c5â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c7, r9c7}: r8c7=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r7c6=X â†’ [r5c6=r6c8], r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r5c6=r6c8], r6c3, r6c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c6) â‰  color(r6c8). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r7c9, r7c6}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r7c9} and {r5c9, r7c6} (non-adjacent).

              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c6, r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r8c3, r9c3â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c7}: r8c3, r8c7â‰ X â†’ r8c5=X

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c9], r5c9, r8c9} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r8c9). Identify.

  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r7c6], [r8c5=r9c7], r9c3, r9c5}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c3, [r5c6=r6c9], r6c6, r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 8, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c3} = {[r6c3=r7c6]}.
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color([r6c3=r7c6]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:

  1.  SET: {row 5, row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 6, col 8, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r6c3, r8c5} = {r7c6, r9c8}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r8c5, r9c4, r9c3}:
      Diagonals: {r6c3, r9c4} and {r8c5, r9c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c3) = color(r9c4) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c8}: r9c4=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r9c4=X â†’ r7c6, r8c5â‰ X

        col 8 {r5c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r5c8, r9c8â‰ X â†’ r8c8=X

        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

      Case 2: color(r8c5) = color(r9c3) = X
        row 8 {r8c5, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c8, r8c9â‰ X

        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r5c6, r7c6â‰ X

        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r6c9, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X

        box 9 {r8c7, r8c9, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c9], r5c9, r8c9} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r6c3, [r5c6=r6c9], r6c6, r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.

      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r7c6], [r8c5=r9c8], r9c3, r9c4}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.

      â†’ color(r9c3) = color([r6c3=r7c6]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

      Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r9c3) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c9}: r9c3=X â†’ r9c4, r9c9â‰ X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c8, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r7c6, r9c4â‰ X â†’ r8c5=X
        r8c5=X â†’ r8c7, r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 9 {r8c7, r8c8, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c9], r5c9, r9c9} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r6c3, [r5c6=r6c9], r6c6, r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.

      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c8=r9c9], [r8c5=r9c3], r8c7, r8c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).

      â†’ color(r9c3) = color([r6c3=r7c6]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

        row 9 {[r5c8=r9c7], r9c3, r9c8}: [r5c8=r9c7]=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
        col 8 {[r5c8=r9c7], r8c8, r9c8}: [r5c8=r9c7]=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c4, r8c5}: r7c6=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c4, r8c5, r8c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r9c3) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r5c8=r9c7], r9c3, r9c8}: r9c3=X â†’ [r5c8=r9c7], r9c8â‰ X
        col 8 {[r5c8=r9c7], r8c8, r9c8}: [r5c8=r9c7], r9c8â‰ X â†’ r8c8=X
        box 6 {[r5c8=r9c7], r5c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
        r8c8=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 5 {r5c6, [r5c8=r9c7], r5c9}: [r5c8=r9c7], r5c9â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.

      â†’ color([r6c3=r7c6]) = color(r8c8). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r7c6=r8c8], r9c3, r9c7, r9c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing) ===

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c8, r9c5:
      Case A: color(r5c8) = color(r9c5). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r5c8=r9c5], r6c9, r6c6, r7c6}:
            Diagonals: {[r5c8=r9c5], r6c6} and {r6c9, r7c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r5c8=r9c5]) = color(r6c6) = X
              row 5 {r5c6, [r5c8=r9c5], r5c9}: [r5c8=r9c5]=X â†’ r5c6, r5c9â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c6=X â†’ r6c3, r6c9â‰ X
              row 9 {[r5c8=r9c5], r9c3, r9c7}: [r5c8=r9c5]=X â†’ r9c3, r9c7â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
              [r5c8=r9c5]=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c3=X â†’ r8c7â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 9 {r8c7, r8c8, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r7c6) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X
              box 6 {[r5c8=r9c5], r5c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ [r5c8=r9c5], r5c9â‰ X
              row 5 {r5c6, [r5c8=r9c5], r5c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c8) â‰  color(r9c5). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c8, r9c5, r7c6}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r9c5} and {r5c8, r7c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r9c5) = X
              row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c7}: r9c5=X â†’ r9c3, r9c7â‰ X
              col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r6c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c9}: r5c8, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X
              r6c9=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              r8c3=X â†’ r8c7, r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 9 {r8c7, r8c8, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r5c8) = color(r7c6) = X
              row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c8=X â†’ r5c6, r5c9â‰ X

              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c6, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

      Diagonals: {r5c6, r7c9} and {r5c9, r7c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r7c9) = X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
        row 7 {r7c6, r7c8, r7c9}: r7c9=X â†’ r7c6, r7c8â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 8 {r5c8, r7c8, r9c8}: r5c8, r7c8â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r7c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r5c9) = color(r7c6) = X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r5c6, r5c8â‰ X
        row 7 {r7c6, r7c8, r7c9}: r7c6=X â†’ r7c8, r7c9â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 8 {r5c8, r7c8, r9c8}: r5c8, r7c8â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        r9c8=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c6, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c9], r5c9, r7c9} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c9, [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r7c9], r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c8=r7c9]).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c8=r7c9], [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c9=r7c6], r6c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c9=r7c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c8=r7c9]) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r5c9=r7c6], [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9], r6c3} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c8=r6c6=r7c9]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r7c6]) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {[r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9], [r5c9=r6c3=r7c6], r7c8} (spine [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9]â€”[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c6=r6c9]) = color(r7c8). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6], [r5c6=r6c9=r7c8], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9], r9c8} (spine [r5c6=r6c9=r7c8]â€”[r5c8=r6c6=r7c9]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r6c3=r7c6]) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  8.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6=r9c8], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 5, row 6, row 7, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 3, col 6, col 8, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c2} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color(r9c2) = color(r8c3), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r7c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r5c9) = color(r7c6) = X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r5c6, r5c8â‰ X
        row 7 {r7c6, r7c7, r7c9}: r7c6=X â†’ r7c7, r7c9â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c7, r7c9, r9c8}: r7c7, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        r9c8=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c6, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c9], r5c9, r7c9} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c9, [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r7c9], r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c8=r7c9]).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c8=r7c9], [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c9=r7c6], r6c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c9=r7c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c8=r7c9]) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r5c9=r7c6], [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9], r6c3} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c8=r6c6=r7c9]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r7c6]) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {[r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9], [r5c9=r6c3=r7c6], r7c7} (spine [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9]â€”[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c6=r6c9]) = color(r7c7). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6], [r5c6=r6c9=r7c7], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c9], r9c8} (spine [r5c6=r6c9=r7c7]â€”[r5c8=r6c6=r7c9]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r6c3=r7c6]) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  8.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6=r9c8], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.

      Remainder: {[r6c3=r7c6]} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color([r6c3=r7c6]) = color(r8c3), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c8=X â†’ r5c6, r5c9â‰ X
        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, r6c9}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X
        row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], r9c3, r9c8}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
        col 3 {[r6c3=r9c2], r8c3, r9c3}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
        col 8 {r5c8, r7c8, r9c8}: r5c8=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X

        r7c6=X and r7c7=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6â‰ X

        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6=X and r7c6=X clash
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).

  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r7c6=r9c8], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 7, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r7c6} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r9c8, r9c2}:

      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r7c7) = color(r9c2) = X

        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 7, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {[r6c3=r7c6]} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color([r6c3=r7c6]) = color(r8c3), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.

  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r7c6=r9c7], r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 7, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r7c6} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r5c8 guards oddagon {r5c8, r5c6, [r7c6=r8c3], r6c3, r6c9} (source r6c6).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r5c8=r6c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r6c9, r6c3, [r7c6=r8c3]} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c6} = {r7c3}.
      â†’ color(r9c6) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r5c8 guards oddagon {r5c8, r5c6, [r7c3=r9c6], r6c3, r6c9} (source r6c6).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r5c8=r6c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, r5c9, r6c9, r6c3, [r7c3=r9c6]} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

pattern 90/832: PROVED cells=13 depth=0 diamonds=0 odd_wheels=0 circular_ladders=0 bridged_hexagons=0 set_equivalences=0 parity_transports=0 pigeonhole_xwings=1 guardians=0 branches=0 complete=true greedy_branches=0 greedy_odd_wheels=0 greedy_circular_ladders=0 greedy_bridged_hexagons=0 greedy_set_equivalences=0 greedy_parity_transports=0 greedy_pigeonhole_xwings=0 greedy_guardians=0 proofs=3
000000000000000000000000000000000000000001011001001001001000000010000110010001001
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r9c9, r9c6}:
      Diagonals: {r5c6, r9c9} and {r5c9, r9c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r9c9) = X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r9c2, r9c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r5c9) = color(r9c6) = X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r5c6, r5c8â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c9}: r9c6=X â†’ r9c2, r9c9â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 9 {r5c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c6, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        r6c3=X â†’ r7c3â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 7 {r7c3, r8c2, r9c2}: r7c3, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r8c2=X
        r8c2=X â†’ r8c7, r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 9 {r8c7, r8c8, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c9} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c9], r5c9, r9c9} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c9, [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r9c9], r9c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c8=r9c9]).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c8=r9c9], [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c9=r9c6], r6c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c9=r9c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c8=r9c9]) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r5c9=r9c6], [r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r9c9], r6c3} (spine [r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r5c8=r6c6=r9c9]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r9c6]) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {[r5c6=r6c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r9c9], [r5c9=r6c3=r9c6], r9c2} (spine [r5c8=r6c6=r9c9]â€”[r5c9=r6c3=r9c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c6=r6c9]) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {[r5c8=r6c6=r9c9], [r5c6=r6c9=r9c2], [r5c9=r6c3=r9c6], r7c3} (spine [r5c6=r6c9=r9c2]â€”[r5c9=r6c3=r9c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c8=r6c6=r9c9]) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  8.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c8=r6c6=r7c3=r9c9], r8c2, r8c7, r8c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c6) = color(r9c3) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c6=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c9}: r9c3=X â†’ r9c5, r9c9â‰ X

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c7} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c7). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c3, [r5c6=r6c7], r6c6, r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c7]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r8c5, [r6c3=r7c6], r9c5, r9c3} (spine [r6c3=r7c6]â€”r9c5).
      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r8c5=r9c3], r8c8, r8c9, r9c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        box 9 {r8c7, r8c9, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        r6c3=X and r9c3=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 8, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c3} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c8, r9c8, [r7c6=r9c3]}:

      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X

        Contradiction.

        Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_oddwheel] (diamond+SET+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r8c9, r9c9, r8c8} (spine r8c9â€”r9c9).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r8c8). Identify.

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c6, r6c7, r9c5, r7c6}:
      Diagonals: {r6c6, r9c5} and {r6c7, r7c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c6) = color(r9c5) = X

        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c7) = color(r7c6) = X

              Contradiction.

              row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r6c7) â‰  color(r9c8). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r6c7, r8c7, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {r6c3, r8c7} and {r6c7, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c7â‰ X

        r9c7=X â†’ r6c7â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c9) = color(r9c3) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r8c3, r8c5â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c7}: r9c3=X â†’ r9c4, r9c7â‰ X

        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, r6c7}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r6c6, r6c7â‰ X
        row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], r9c3, r9c8}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
        col 3 {[r6c3=r9c2], r8c3, r9c3}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
        r7c6=X â†’ r7c7, r7c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 9 {r7c7, r7c9, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c7) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], r9c3, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
        col 3 {[r6c3=r9c2], r8c3, r9c3}: [r6c3=r9c2], r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c7, r7c9, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r7c7, r7c9â‰ X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c8, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        row 7 {r7c6, r7c7, r7c9}: r7c7, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r5c6=X and r7c6=X clash
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c7} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c7). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c3, [r5c6=r6c7], r6c6, r7c6} (spine [r5c6=r6c7]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r6c6, [r5c6=r6c7], [r6c3=r7c6], r7c7} (spine [r5c6=r6c7]â€”[r6c3=r7c6]).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r7c7). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c6=r6c7], [r6c3=r7c6], [r6c6=r7c7], r7c9} (spine [r6c3=r7c6]â€”[r6c6=r7c7]).
      â†’ color([r5c6=r6c7]) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {r5c9, [r5c6=r6c7=r7c9], r5c8, r9c8} (spine [r5c6=r6c7=r7c9]â€”r5c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r6c7=r7c9], [r5c9=r9c8], [r6c6=r7c7]} (spine [r5c6=r6c7=r7c9]â€”[r5c9=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color([r6c6=r7c7]). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {[r5c9=r9c8], [r5c6=r6c7=r7c9], [r5c8=r6c6=r7c7], [r6c3=r7c6]} (spine [r5c6=r6c7=r7c9]â€”[r5c8=r6c6=r7c7]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r9c8]) = color([r6c3=r7c6]). Identify.
  8.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c9=r6c3=r7c6=r9c8], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 7, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r7c6} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r6c7, r7c7, [r7c6=r8c3]}:
      Diagonals: {r6c3, r7c7} and {r6c7, [r7c6=r8c3]} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c3) = color(r7c7) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c7â‰ X
        row 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r7c9}: r7c7=X â†’ [r7c6=r8c3], r7c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r7c6=r8c3], r9c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]}: r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        box 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r9c2, r9c3}: [r7c6=r8c3], r9c3â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
        box 9 {r7c7, r7c9, r9c8}: r7c7=X â†’ r9c8â‰ X
        r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c7) = color([r7c6=r8c3]) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
        row 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r7c9}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r7c7, r7c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r7c6=r8c3], r9c3}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
        box 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r9c2, r9c3}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c7, r7c9, r9c8}: r7c7, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c7) = color([r7c6=r8c3]) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
        row 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r7c9}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r7c7, r7c9â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
        box 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r9c1, r9c2}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c7, r7c9, r9c8}: r7c7, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
        row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===

  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {[r6c3=r9c6]} = {r7c3}.
      â†’ color([r6c3=r9c6]) = color(r7c3), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===

      â†’ color(r8c2) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r6c7, [r8c2=r9c7], r7c3}:
      Diagonals: {r6c3, [r8c2=r9c7]} and {r6c7, r7c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c3) = color([r8c2=r9c7]) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c7â‰ X

              row 8 {r8c3, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c3, r8c9â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, [r6c5=r9c3], r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r6c5) â‰  color(r9c3). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r6c5, r9c5, r7c6}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r9c5} and {r6c5, r7c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r9c5) = X
              row 5 {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c6=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r5c8, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
              r5c6=X â†’ r6c5, r7c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c7=X â†’ r6c3, r7c7â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c5=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              r8c3=X â†’ r8c8, r8c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 9 {r7c7, r8c8, r8c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c5) = color(r7c6) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c5, r6c7}: r6c5=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X
              r6c5=X â†’ r5c6, r9c3, r9c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              r7c6=X â†’ r7c7â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              r8c3=X â†’ r8c8, r8c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 9 {r7c7, r8c8, r8c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c7} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c7). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c7, r8c8, r8c9, r8c3} (spine r8c8â€”r8c9).
      â†’ color(r7c7) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r6c5, [r5c6=r6c7], r6c3, [r7c7=r8c3]} (spine [r5c6=r6c7]â€”r6c3).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color([r7c7=r8c3]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c5=r7c7=r8c3] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {[r5c6=r6c7], r6c3, r9c3, r9c5, r7c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        col 7 {[r5c6=r6c7], [r8c3=r9c7], r8c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c7} (spine r5c8â€”r5c9).

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c6, r6c7:
      Case A: color(r5c6) = color(r6c7). Identify.
        2.  Guardian: r8c3 guards oddagon {r8c3, r8c7, [r5c6=r6c7], r9c6, r9c2} (source r9c7).
            â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c7). Identify.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r7c5, r7c9, [r8c3=r9c7], r9c6}:
            Diagonals: {r7c5, [r8c3=r9c7]} and {r7c9, r9c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r7c5) = color([r8c3=r9c7]) = X
              row 9 {[r8c3=r9c7], r9c2, r9c6}: [r8c3=r9c7]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c6â‰ X
              col 7 {[r5c6=r6c7], [r8c3=r9c7], r8c7}: [r8c3=r9c7]=X â†’ [r5c6=r6c7], r8c7â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, [r8c3=r9c7], r8c7}: [r8c3=r9c7]=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r7c5=X â†’ r6c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              [r8c3=r9c7]=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r5c6=r6c7], r6c3, r6c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c9) = color(r9c6) = X
              row 9 {[r8c3=r9c7], r9c2, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ [r8c3=r9c7], r9c2â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, [r8c3=r9c7], r8c7}: r7c9=X â†’ r8c7â‰ X
              r7c9=X â†’ r5c9, r7c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c6=X â†’ [r5c6=r6c7]â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 5 {[r5c6=r6c7], r5c8, r5c9}: [r5c6=r6c7], r5c9â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X
              col 7 {[r5c6=r6c7], [r8c3=r9c7], r8c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c6) â‰  color(r6c7). Add edge.
        4.  Kâ‚„ on {r5c6, r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel+guardian) ===

        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c8, r8c4, r8c5, r9c5} (spine r8c4â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r8c8) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c9, [r8c8=r9c5], r9c8, r9c3} (spine [r8c8=r9c5]â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r7c9) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c6, r5c3, r5c9, [r7c9=r9c3]} (spine r5c3â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color([r7c9=r9c3]). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c6=r7c9=r9c3], r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET+ladder) ===

      Remainder: {r9c3} = {r7c9}.
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color(r7c9), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+ladder) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r7c9, r8c7, r8c8, r8c5} (spine r8c7â€”r8c8).

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c5, r8c8, r8c9, r9c9} (spine r8c8â€”r8c9).
      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c9, [r8c5=r9c9], r8c9, r8c8} (spine [r8c5=r9c9]â€”r8c9).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r8c8). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r7c6, [r8c5=r9c9], r9c5, r9c3} (spine [r8c5=r9c9]â€”r9c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c9=r8c8], r5c3, r5c6, [r7c6=r9c3]} (spine r5c3â€”r5c6).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r8c8]) = color([r7c6=r9c3]). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c9=r7c6=r8c8=r9c3], r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET+ladder) ===

  2.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 8, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c3} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color(r7c6), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c9=r7c6=r9c3], r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET+ladder) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r5c3â€”r6c3, r5c6â€”r6c6, r5c9â€”r6c8}:
      Satellites r9c3 (rung 1), r7c6 (rung 2) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  2.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 8, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c3} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color(r7c6), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c3, r5c9, r6c8, r6c3}:
      Diagonals: {r5c3, r6c8} and {r5c9, r6c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c3) = color(r6c8) = X
        row 5 {r5c3, r5c6, r5c9}: r5c3=X â†’ r5c6, r5c9â‰ X

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_parity] (ladder+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r5c3â€”r6c3, r5c6â€”r6c6, r5c9â€”r6c8}:
      Satellites r9c3 (rung 1), r7c6 (rung 2) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r6c6, r7c6, r9c3}:

      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c3) = color(r7c6) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c8â‰ X

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r7c9, r9c9, r8c7} (spine r7c9â€”r9c9).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r8c7). Identify.
  2.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
      row 6: r6c6, r6c8 (also r6c3)
      row 5: r5c6, [r5c9=r8c7] (also r5c3)
      row 9: r9c4, r9c9 (also r9c3)
      row 7: r7c6, r7c9
      row 8: r8c5, [r5c9=r8c7]
      Rows 5 and 6: r5c6â†”r6c6 (col 6), r5c9â†”r6c8 (box 6), r5c3â†”r6c3 (col 3) â€” derangement â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 9, 7, 8: {r9c4, r7c6, r8c5} in box 8, {r9c9, r7c9, r8c7} in box 9 â€” circular ladder of length 3 â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 5 and 9: r5c9â†”r9c9 (col 9), r5c3â†”r9c3 (col 3) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r5c6 and r9c4).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 5, row 6, row 7, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 5, row 6, row 7, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r5â†”r6: r5c6â†”r6c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r5â†”r7: r5c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r5â†”r9: r5c9â†”r9c9 (col 9) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r7: r6c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r9: r6c3â†”r9c3 (col 3) â†’ same Î´, pairs differ.
          r7â†”r9: r7c6â†”r9c4 (box 8) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r5c6) = color(r9c4). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {[r5c6=r9c4], r5c3, r9c3, r6c3} (spine r5c3â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color([r5c6=r9c4]) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r5c6=r6c3=r9c4] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {[r5c9=r8c7], r6c8, r6c6, r7c6, r8c5} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Parity chain deduction:
      row 5 {r5c3, r5c6, r5c9}

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_parity] (diamond+xwing+branch) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r6c3, r8c5:
      Case A: color(r6c3) = color(r8c5). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c9, r6c8, r8c8, r7c9}:

              r6c8=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 8 {[r6c3=r8c5], r8c3, r8c8}: [r6c3=r8c5], r8c8â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              col 3 {r5c3, [r6c3=r8c5], r8c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r5c3â‰ X
              r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 5 {r5c3, r5c6, r5c9}: r5c3, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c2, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              r5c6=X and r9c6=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r6c3) â‰  color(r8c5). Add edge.
        3.  Diamond {r6c5, r6c3, r8c5, r8c3} (spine r6c3â€”r8c5).
            â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r8c3). Identify.
        4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, [r6c5=r8c3], r8c5, r9c6}:

              r6c3=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c3=X and r8c5=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color([r6c5=r8c3]) = color(r9c6) = X

      row 5: [r5c6=r8c4], r5c9 (also r5c3)
      row 9: r9c6, r9c7 (also r9c3)
      row 7: r7c6, r7c8
      row 8: [r5c6=r8c4], r8c7
      Rows 5 and 6: r5c6â†”r6c5 (box 5), r5c9â†”r6c8 (box 6), r5c3â†”r6c3 (col 3) â€” derangement â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 9, 7, 8: {r9c6, r7c6, r8c4} in box 8, {r9c7, r7c8, r8c7} in box 9 â€” circular ladder of length 3 â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 5 and 9: r5c6â†”r9c6 (col 6), r5c3â†”r9c3 (col 3) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r5c9 and r9c7).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 5, row 6, row 7, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 5, row 6, row 7, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r5â†”r6: r5c6â†”r6c5 (box 5) â†’ pairs differ.
          r5â†”r7: r5c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r5â†”r9: r5c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r7: r6c8â†”r7c8 (col 8) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r9: r6c3â†”r9c3 (col 3) â†’ same Î´, pairs differ.
          r7â†”r9: r7c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r5c9) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {[r5c9=r9c7], r5c3, r9c3, r6c3} (spine r5c3â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r9c7]) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r5c9=r6c3=r9c7] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {[r5c6=r8c4], r6c5, r6c8, r7c8, r8c7} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_parity] (xwing+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c9, r6c5:
      Case A: color(r5c9) = color(r6c5). Identify.
        2.  Guardian: r9c6 guards oddagon {r9c6, r5c6, [r5c9=r6c5], r6c3, r9c3} (source r5c3).
            â†’ color(r9c6) = color(r5c3). Identify.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r5c3=r9c6], r7c6, r7c8, r9c7}:
            Diagonals: {[r5c3=r9c6], r7c8} and {r7c6, r9c7} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r5c3=r9c6]) = color(r7c8) = X
              row 5 {[r5c3=r9c6], r5c6, [r5c9=r6c5]}: [r5c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r5c6, [r5c9=r6c5]â‰ X
              row 9 {[r5c3=r9c6], r9c3, r9c7}: [r5c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c3, r9c7â‰ X
              col 3 {[r5c3=r9c6], r6c3, r9c3}: [r5c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
              col 6 {[r5c3=r9c6], r5c6, r7c6}: [r5c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
              box 8 {[r5c3=r9c6], r7c6, r8c4}: [r5c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r8c4â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c7}: r7c8=X â†’ r8c7â‰ X
              r7c8=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r5c9=r6c5], r6c3, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c6) = color(r9c7) = X
              row 9 {[r5c3=r9c6], r9c3, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ [r5c3=r9c6], r9c3â‰ X
              col 3 {[r5c3=r9c6], r6c3, r9c3}: [r5c3=r9c6], r9c3â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 6 {[r5c3=r9c6], r5c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X
              box 8 {[r5c3=r9c6], r7c6, r8c4}: r7c6=X â†’ r8c4â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ r7c8, r8c7â‰ X
              r6c3=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c5], r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 5 {[r5c3=r9c6], r5c6, [r5c9=r6c5]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c9) â‰  color(r6c5). Add edge.
        4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r7c6, r7c8, r8c7, r8c4}:
            Diagonals: {r7c6, r8c7} and {r7c8, r8c4} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r7c6) = color(r8c7) = X
              col 6 {r5c6, r7c6, r9c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r5c6, r9c6â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c4, r9c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r8c4â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c7}: r8c7=X â†’ r7c8, r9c7â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c6, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c3=X
              col 3 {r5c3, r6c3, r9c3}: r9c3=X â†’ r5c3, r6c3â‰ X
              row 5 {r5c3, r5c6, r5c9}: r5c3, r5c6â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
              r5c9=X â†’ r6c5, r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c5, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c8) = color(r8c4) = X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c4, r9c6}: r8c4=X â†’ r7c6, r9c6â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c7}: r7c8=X â†’ r8c7, r9c7â‰ X
              r7c8=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c6, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c3=X
              col 3 {r5c3, r6c3, r9c3}: r9c3=X â†’ r5c3, r6c3â‰ X
              col 6 {r5c6, r7c6, r9c6}: r7c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
              r5c6=X â†’ r5c9, r6c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c5, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+parity+branch) ===

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r7c9, r9c9, r8c8} (spine r7c9â€”r9c9).

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_oddwheel] (diamond+xwing+branch) ===

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_oddwheel] (diamond+xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r7c3, r9c9:
      Case A: color(r7c3) = color(r9c9). Identify.

              r8c2=X and r8c8=X are adjacent

              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r7c3) â‰  color(r9c9). Add edge.

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_diamond] (xwing+oddwheel+branch+guardian) ===

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r4c9, r5c9, r7c9} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c5, r5c6, r6c6, r9c6} (spine r5c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {[r6c5=r9c6]} = {[r6c8=r7c9]}.
      â†’ color([r6c5=r9c6]) = color([r6c8=r7c9]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r6c8, r6c5}:
      Diagonals: {r5c6, r6c8} and {r5c9, r6c5} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r6c8) = X
        row 6 {r6c5, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c5, r6c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r9c6â‰ X
        box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
        r6c8=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 9 {r4c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r4c9, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r7c9=X
        box 9 {r7c9, r8c7, r8c8}: r7c9=X â†’ r8c7â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c7, r8c8}: r8c7, r8c8â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r4c9, r5c9, r7c9} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c5, r5c6, r6c6, r9c6} (spine r5c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r9c6). Identify.

      â†’ color(r5c6) = color([r6c8=r7c9]). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c6=r6c8=r7c9], r8c7, r8c8, r8c3} (spine r8c7â€”r8c8).
      â†’ color([r5c6=r6c8=r7c9]) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c6=r6c8=r7c9=r8c3], [r6c5=r9c6], r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r5c9) = color(r6c5) = X
        row 6 {r6c5, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c5=X â†’ r6c6, r6c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r4c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r4c9, r7c9â‰ X

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r4c9, r5c9, r7c9} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c5, r5c6, r6c6, r8c6} (spine r5c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c6, [r6c5=r8c6], r6c6, [r6c8=r7c9]} (spine [r6c5=r8c6]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color([r6c8=r7c9]). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r6c6, [r5c6=r6c8=r7c9], [r6c5=r8c6], r8c7} (spine [r5c6=r6c8=r7c9]â€”[r6c5=r8c6]).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r8c7). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r6c5=r8c6], [r5c6=r6c8=r7c9], [r6c6=r8c7], r9c7} (spine [r5c6=r6c8=r7c9]â€”[r6c6=r8c7]).
      â†’ color([r6c5=r8c6]) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c5=r8c6=r9c7], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color([r6c5=r8c6]) = color([r6c8=r7c9]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  4.  Diamond {r6c6, [r5c6=r6c8=r8c9], [r6c5=r7c6], r8c5} (spine [r5c6=r6c8=r8c9]â€”[r6c5=r7c6]).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r8c5). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r6c5=r7c6], [r5c6=r6c8=r8c9], [r6c6=r8c5], r8c3} (spine [r5c6=r6c8=r8c9]â€”[r6c6=r8c5]).
      â†’ color([r6c5=r7c6]) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c5=r7c6=r8c3], r9c1, r9c2, r9c4}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r4c9, r5c9, r8c9} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c5, r5c6, r6c6, r7c6} (spine r5c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {[r6c8=r8c9]} = {[r6c5=r7c6]}.
      â†’ color([r6c8=r8c9]) = color([r6c5=r7c6]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r8c3) = color(r9c6) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X

      â†’ color(r8c6) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {r8c3, [r8c6=r9c7], [r8c8=r9c6], r9c2}. Contradiction.

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r4c9, r5c9, r9c9} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r8c9} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r6c5 guards oddagon {r6c5, r5c6, [r7c6=r8c9], r4c9, r6c8} (source r5c9).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r5c9). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r5c9=r6c5], r6c3, r8c3, [r7c6=r8c9]}:
      Diagonals: {[r5c9=r6c5], r8c3} and {r6c3, [r7c6=r8c9]} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r5c9=r6c5]) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 6 {[r5c9=r6c5], r6c3, r6c8}: [r5c9=r6c5]=X â†’ r6c3, r6c8â‰ X
        row 8 {[r7c6=r8c9], r8c3, r8c6}: r8c3=X â†’ [r7c6=r8c9], r8c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, [r7c6=r8c9], r8c6}: [r7c6=r8c9], r8c6â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        col 9 {r4c9, [r5c9=r6c5], [r7c6=r8c9]}: [r5c9=r6c5]=X â†’ r4c9â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
        box 8 {[r7c6=r8c9], r8c6, r9c4}: [r7c6=r8c9], r8c6â‰ X â†’ r9c4=X
        r5c6=X and [r5c9=r6c5]=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c3) = color([r7c6=r8c9]) = X
        row 6 {[r5c9=r6c5], r6c3, r6c8}: r6c3=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c5], r6c8â‰ X

        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Diamond {[r5c8=r9c3], r4c9, [r6c7=r8c3], r8c9} (spine r4c9â€”[r6c7=r8c3]).
      â†’ color([r5c8=r9c3]) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {r8c6, [r5c6=r9c5], [r5c8=r8c9=r9c3], r9c8} (spine [r5c6=r9c5]â€”[r5c8=r8c9=r9c3]).
      â†’ color(r8c6) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r5c8=r8c9=r9c3], [r6c7=r8c3], r7c7, [r8c6=r9c8]}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (ladder+hexagon) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c5, r8c9â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r7c6 (rung 2), r7c7 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Circular ladder {r6c3â€”r8c3, r7c6â€”r8c6, r7c7â€”r8c9}:
      Satellites r9c3 (rung 1), r5c6 (rung 2), r9c8 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Bridged hexagon: ring {r5c6, r5c8, r6c7, r6c3, r8c3, r8c6}
      Bridges: r9c8â€”r9c3 (edges r5c6â€”r5c8 â†” r6c3â€”r8c3), r4c9â€”r8c9 (edges r5c8â€”r6c7 â†” r8c3â€”r8c6), r7c7â€”r7c6 (edges r6c7â€”r6c3 â†” r8c6â€”r5c6).
      Each bridge forces opposite edges to miss different colors,
      but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_ladder] (diamond+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r7c6, r8c6, r9c5} (spine r7c6â€”r8c6).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r5c8, [r5c6=r9c5], r9c8, r9c3} (spine [r5c6=r9c5]â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r4c9, [r5c8=r9c3], r6c7, r6c3} (spine [r5c8=r9c3]â€”r6c7).
      â†’ color(r4c9) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r4c9=r6c3], r6c7, r7c7, r8c9}:
      Diagonals: {[r4c9=r6c3], r7c7} and {r6c7, r8c9} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r4c9=r6c3]) = color(r7c7) = X
        col 3 {[r4c9=r6c3], [r5c8=r9c3], r8c3}: [r4c9=r6c3]=X â†’ [r5c8=r9c3], r8c3â‰ X
        box 6 {[r4c9=r6c3], [r5c8=r9c3], r6c7}: [r4c9=r6c3]=X â†’ r6c7â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c7, r8c9, r9c8}: r7c7=X â†’ r8c9, r9c8â‰ X
        r7c7=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c3, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r8c6=X
        row 9 {[r5c6=r9c5], [r5c8=r9c3], r9c8}: [r5c8=r9c3], r9c8â‰ X â†’ [r5c6=r9c5]=X
        col 6 {[r5c6=r9c5], r7c6, r8c6}: [r5c6=r9c5]=X and r8c6=X clash
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c7) = color(r8c9) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
        box 6 {[r4c9=r6c3], [r5c8=r9c3], r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ [r4c9=r6c3], [r5c8=r9c3]â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c7, r8c9, r9c8}: r8c9=X â†’ r7c7, r9c8â‰ X
        row 9 {[r5c6=r9c5], [r5c8=r9c3], r9c8}: [r5c8=r9c3], r9c8â‰ X â†’ [r5c6=r9c5]=X
        col 3 {[r4c9=r6c3], [r5c8=r9c3], r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      Case A: color(r4c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r4c9=r9c8], r8c9, r8c3, r9c3}:

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, [r5c8=r8c9], r6c9, r6c3, r7c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              col 9 {[r4c9=r9c7], r6c9, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
              box 6 {[r4c9=r9c7], r5c8, r6c9}: [r4c9=r9c7], r6c9â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X

              r5c8=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r7c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r4c9) â‰  color(r9c7). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c5, r8c9, r9c7, r9c5}:
            Diagonals: {r8c5, r9c7} and {r8c9, r9c5} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r4c9, r6c9, r8c9} (spine r4c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {[r5c8=r8c9]â€”r9c7, r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c5â€”r9c5}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 2), r7c6 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  3.  Circular ladder {r6c3â€”r7c6, r8c3â€”r8c5, r9c3â€”r9c5}:
      Satellites r6c6 (rung 1), [r5c8=r8c9] (rung 2), r9c7 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub r6c6 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, [r5c8=r8c9], r6c9, r6c3, r7c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r4c9, r6c9, r8c9} (spine r4c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {[r5c8=r8c9]â€”r9c7, r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c5â€”r9c4}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 2), r7c6 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  3.  Circular ladder {r6c3â€”r7c6, r8c3â€”r8c5, r9c3â€”r9c4}:
      Satellites r6c6 (rung 1), [r5c8=r8c9] (rung 2), r9c7 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub r6c6 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c6, [r5c8=r8c9], r6c9, r6c3, r7c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c5â€”r9c5, r8c9â€”r9c9}:

=== Greedy proof (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r7c6, r8c6, r9c5} (spine r7c6â€”r8c6).

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Kâ‚„ on {r4c9, [r5c6=r9c5], r5c8, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_ladder] (xwing+branch) ===

              Contradiction.

              col 9 {[r4c9=r6c5], r8c9, r9c9}: [r4c9=r6c5], r8c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X

=== Greedy proof (diamond+parity) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).

      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Gradient chain (with identification):

        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing [r6c3=r9c2] and [r6c3=r9c2]).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 6, row 7, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.

              [r4c9=r6c5]=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c9}: r8c5, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X

              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r4c9) â‰  color(r6c5). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c5, r9c4, r9c3}:
            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c4} and {r8c5, r9c3} (non-adjacent).

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_oddwheel] (diamond+ladder+xwing) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c5â€”r9c4, r8c9â€”r9c9}:

=== Greedy proof (diamond+parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r4c9, r7c9, r8c9, r9c8} (spine r7c9â€”r8c9).
      â†’ color(r4c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r6c7, [r4c9=r9c8], [r6c3=r9c2], r9c6} (spine [r4c9=r9c8]â€”[r6c3=r9c2]).
      â†’ color(r6c7) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
      row 6: [r6c3=r9c2], [r6c7=r9c6] (also r6c5)
      row 9: [r6c3=r9c2], [r4c9=r9c8] (also [r6c7=r9c6])
      row 7: r7c3, r7c9
      row 8: r8c3, r8c9
      Rows 9, 7, 8: {r9c2, r7c3, r8c3} in box 7, {r9c8, r7c9, r8c9} in box 9 â€” circular ladder of length 3 â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 6 and 9: r6c7â†”r9c8 (box 6), r6c5â†”r9c6 (box 5) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing [r6c3=r9c2] and [r6c3=r9c2]).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 6, row 7, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 6, row 7, row 8, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r6â†”r7: r6c3â†”r7c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r8: r6c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.

  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r4c9, r6c6, r8c6, r8c9}:
      Diagonals: {r4c9, r8c6} and {r6c6, r8c9} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r4c9) = color(r8c6) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c6=X â†’ r8c3, r8c9â‰ X

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r8c6). Identify.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r4c9, r7c9, r8c9, r9c8} (spine r7c9â€”r8c9).
      â†’ color(r4c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 5, row 6}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 5, box 6}.
      Remainder: {[r6c3=r9c2]} = {[r4c9=r9c8]}.
      â†’ color([r6c3=r9c2]) = color([r4c9=r9c8]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              r8c5=X â†’ r5c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 5 {[r4c9=r5c6], r5c5, r5c8}: [r4c9=r5c6], r5c5â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X
              col 6 {[r4c9=r5c6], r6c6, r7c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c9) = color(r9c4) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r8c3, r8c5â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c9}: r9c4=X â†’ r9c3, r9c9â‰ X

        box 5 {r5c5, r5c6, r6c6}: r5c5=X and r6c6=X clash
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c6) = color(r9c7) = X
        row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], r9c6, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ [r6c3=r9c2], r9c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r5c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X
        box 5 {r5c5, r5c6, r6c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r5c5â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c9, r8c9, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ r7c9, r8c9â‰ X
        row 5 {r5c5, r5c6, r5c8}: r5c5, r5c6â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X
        col 9 {r4c9, r7c9, r8c9}: r7c9, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r4c9=X
        r4c9=X and r5c8=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r5c5, r5c6, r6c6} (spine r5c5â€”r5c6).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c6). Identify.

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c5, r7c3:
      Case A: color(r5c5) = color(r7c3). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r4c9, r5c8, [r5c5=r7c3], r7c9}:

            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r5c5, r5c6, r6c4} (spine r5c5â€”r5c6).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c4). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c5, r8c9â€”r9c9}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r7c4 (rung 2), r4c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Kâ‚„ on {r4c9, [r5c8=r6c4], r6c3, r7c4}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (ladder+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c5, r8c9â€”r9c9}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r7c4 (rung 2), r4c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Branch on r4c9, r6c4:
      Case A: color(r4c9) = color(r6c4). Identify.
        3.  Kâ‚„ on {[r4c9=r6c4], r5c5, r5c6, r5c8}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r4c9) â‰  color(r6c4). Add edge.
        4.  Kâ‚„ on {r4c9, r6c3, r6c4, r7c4}. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_ladder] (diamond+xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r5c5, r5c6, r6c4} (spine r5c5â€”r5c6).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c4). Identify.
  2.  Branch on r4c9, r8c3:
      Case A: color(r4c9) = color(r8c3). Identify.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r4c9=r8c3], [r5c8=r6c4], r7c4, r8c6}:
            Diagonals: {[r4c9=r8c3], r7c4} and {[r5c8=r6c4], r8c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r4c9=r8c3]) = color(r7c4) = X
              row 8 {[r4c9=r8c3], r8c6, r8c9}: [r4c9=r8c3]=X â†’ r8c6, r8c9â‰ X
              col 3 {[r4c9=r8c3], r6c3, r9c3}: [r4c9=r8c3]=X â†’ r6c3, r9c3â‰ X
              col 9 {[r4c9=r8c3], r8c9, r9c9}: [r4c9=r8c3]=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c4, r8c6, r9c5}: r7c4=X â†’ r9c5â‰ X
              [r4c9=r8c3]=X â†’ [r5c8=r6c4]â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color([r5c8=r6c4]) = color(r8c6) = X
              row 5 {r5c5, r5c6, [r5c8=r6c4]}: [r5c8=r6c4]=X â†’ r5c5, r5c6â‰ X
              row 8 {[r4c9=r8c3], r8c6, r8c9}: r8c6=X â†’ [r4c9=r8c3], r8c9â‰ X
              col 9 {[r4c9=r8c3], r8c9, r9c9}: [r4c9=r8c3], r8c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X
              box 8 {r7c4, r8c6, r9c5}: r8c6=X â†’ r7c4, r9c5â‰ X
              [r5c8=r6c4]=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c9=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {[r4c9=r8c3], r6c3, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r4c9) â‰  color(r8c3). Add edge.
        4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r4c9, [r5c8=r6c4], r6c3, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {r4c9, r6c3} and {[r5c8=r6c4], r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r4c9) = color(r6c3) = X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r8c3, r9c3â‰ X
              col 9 {r4c9, r8c9, r9c9}: r4c9=X â†’ r8c9, r9c9â‰ X
              r4c9=X â†’ [r5c8=r6c4]â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c3, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r8c6=X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c9}: r9c3, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c5=X
              box 8 {r7c4, r8c6, r9c5}: r8c6=X and r9c5=X clash
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color([r5c8=r6c4]) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 5 {r5c5, r5c6, [r5c8=r6c4]}: [r5c8=r6c4]=X â†’ r5c5, r5c6â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c9â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r6c3, r9c3â‰ X
              [r5c8=r6c4]=X â†’ r4c9, r7c4â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r4c9, r8c9, r9c9}: r4c9, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X
              box 8 {r7c4, r8c6, r9c5}: r7c4, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r9c5=X
              r9c5=X and r9c9=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r3c9, r6c9, r6c8}:
      Diagonals: {r3c8, r6c9} and {r3c9, r6c8} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c8) = color(r6c9) = X
        row 6 {r6c6, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c6, r6c8â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r7c8}: r3c8=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r3c9, r7c9â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c8, r7c9, r8c7}: r7c8, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r8c7=X
        r8c7=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 6 {r6c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r6c6, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
        r9c6=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X
        row 6 {r6c6, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r7c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r3c8, r7c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X

        r8c7=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X (adjacent)

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:

  2.  Diamond {r6c6, [r8c3=r9c6], r8c6, r8c7} (spine [r8c3=r9c6]â€”r8c6).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r8c7). Identify.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Diamond {r3c8, [r3c9=r9c8], [r5c8=r8c9], r5c9} (spine [r3c9=r9c8]â€”[r5c8=r8c9]).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color(r5c9). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r3c9=r9c8], [r3c8=r5c9], [r5c8=r8c9], r6c7} (spine [r3c8=r5c9]â€”[r5c8=r8c9]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r9c8]) = color(r6c7). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {r6c6, [r3c9=r6c7=r9c8], r6c3, r9c3} (spine [r3c9=r6c7=r9c8]â€”r6c3).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {r6c3, [r3c9=r6c7=r9c8], [r6c6=r9c3], r9c6} (spine [r3c9=r6c7=r9c8]â€”[r6c6=r9c3]).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  8.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r9c6], [r6c6=r9c3], r8c3, r8c6}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r6c3â€”r6c6, r8c3â€”r8c6, r9c3â€”r9c6}:
      Satellites r6c7 (rung 1), r8c9 (rung 2), r9c8 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Diamond {r5c9, r5c8, r6c7, r9c8} (spine r5c8â€”r6c7).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r5c9=r9c8] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c8, r3c9, r8c9, r6c7, r5c8} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.
