        box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r8c3=X and r9c2=X clash
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r7c3) = X
        col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r7c3=X â†’ r6c3, r8c3â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r6c9, r7c9â‰ X
        box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
        r3c9=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c3, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
        col 8 {r6c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r8c8, r9c8â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r8c8, r9c8, r7c9} (spine r8c8â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c2, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r7c3 (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), [r6c8=r7c9] (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c8=r7c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c9, r6c3, r7c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+ladder+parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {[r6c3=r9c2]â€”r9c7, r6c8â€”r8c8, r6c9â€”r7c9}:
      Satellites r9c6 (rung 1), r3c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  3.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, r9c6, r8c6} (spine r3c6â€”r9c6).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  4.  Trivalue oddagon:
      row 6 {[r6c3=r9c2], r6c8, r6c9}
        â†’ via cols {8, 9} [odd]
      box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c7}
        â†’ via rows {7, 8} [even]
      col 3 {[r6c3=r9c2], r7c3, r8c3}
        â†’ via rows {6, 7} [even]
      col 9 {[r3c9=r8c6], r6c9, r7c9}
        â†’ via boxes {9} [odd]
      row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], r9c6, r9c7}
        â†’ via boxes {7, 9} [odd]
      row 8 {[r3c9=r8c6], r8c3, r8c8}
        â†’ via cols {3, 8} [even]
      Cycle parity: odd. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (ladder+oddwheel+guardian) ===

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r7c9). Identify.

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_ladder] (xwing+branch) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r8c3:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r8c3], r6c3, r6c8, r8c8}:

              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c2, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X

              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c7}: r7c9=X and r9c7=X clash
              Contradiction.

              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c8, r6c9â‰ X

              col 6 {[r3c6=r8c3], r8c6, r9c6}: [r3c6=r8c3], r8c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X

              r9c2=X and r9c6=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r8c3). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c8, r9c7, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c7} and {r8c8, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

        box 7 {r7c3, r8c2, r9c1}: r7c3=X â†’ r8c2, r9c1â‰ X
        r3c9=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        r7c3=X â†’ r6c3, r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+ladder) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r8c8, r9c8, r7c9} (spine r8c8â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c9, r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], r7c3} (spine r6c3â€”[r6c8=r7c9]).
      â†’ color(r6c9) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c9, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3], r6c3} (spine [r6c8=r7c9]â€”[r6c9=r7c3]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  4.  Circular ladder {[r6c8=r7c9]â€”[r6c9=r7c3], r8c8â€”r8c2, r9c8â€”r9c1}:
      Satellites [r3c9=r6c3] (rung 1), r8c6 (rung 2), r9c6 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {r3c6, [r3c9=r6c3], r8c6, r9c6}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_ladder] (diamond+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r8c8, r9c8, r7c9} (spine r8c8â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c9, r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], r7c3} (spine r6c3â€”[r6c8=r7c9]).
      â†’ color(r6c9) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c2, r8c8, r9c8, r9c1}:
      Diagonals: {r8c2, r9c8} and {r8c8, r9c1} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r8c2) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 8 {r8c2, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c2=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c1, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c1, r9c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 8 {[r6c8=r7c9], r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ [r6c8=r7c9]â‰ X
        box 7 {[r6c9=r7c3], r8c2, r9c1}: r8c2=X â†’ [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X
        r3c6=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        col 9 {r3c9, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c8) = color(r9c1) = X
        row 8 {r8c2, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c2, r8c6â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c1, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c1=X â†’ r9c6, r9c8â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 8 {[r6c8=r7c9], r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8=X â†’ [r6c8=r7c9]â‰ X
        box 7 {[r6c9=r7c3], r8c2, r9c1}: r9c1=X â†’ [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X
        r3c6=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        col 9 {r3c9, [r6c8=r7c9], [r6c9=r7c3]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c8, r8c8, r9c8, r7c9} (spine r8c8â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r8c2â€”r9c1, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r7c3 (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), [r6c8=r7c9] (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c8=r7c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c9, r6c3, r7c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        box 6 {r5c9, r6c8, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8]) = color(r9c6). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8=r9c6]) = color(r7c8), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  2.  Guardian: r6c6 guards oddagon {r6c6, r6c8, r7c8, [r8c3=r9c7], r8c6} (source r8c8).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r8c8). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c9, r6c9, [r6c6=r8c8]}:
      Diagonals: {r3c6, r6c9} and {r3c9, [r6c6=r8c8]} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r6c9) = X
        row 6 {[r6c6=r8c8], r6c8, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ [r6c6=r8c8], r6c8â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r6c6=r8c8], r8c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r8c6â‰ X
        col 8 {[r6c6=r8c8], r6c8, r7c8}: [r6c6=r8c8], r6c8â‰ X â†’ r7c8=X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X
        box 9 {[r6c6=r8c8], r7c8, [r8c3=r9c7]}: r7c8=X â†’ [r8c3=r9c7]â‰ X
        row 8 {[r6c6=r8c8], [r8c3=r9c7], r8c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c9) = color([r6c6=r8c8]) = X
        row 6 {[r6c6=r8c8], r6c8, r6c9}: [r6c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r6c8, r6c9â‰ X
        row 8 {[r6c6=r8c8], [r8c3=r9c7], r8c6}: [r6c6=r8c8]=X â†’ [r8c3=r9c7], r8c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r6c6=r8c8], r8c6}: [r6c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
        col 8 {[r6c6=r8c8], r6c8, r7c8}: [r6c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r6c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c9, r6c8, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8]) = color(r8c6). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8=r8c6]) = color(r7c8), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r6c8:

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r5c9, r6c9, r6c8} (spine r5c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8]) = color(r7c6). Identify.

      â†’ color(r8c8) = color([r3c9=r6c8=r7c6]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r5c9, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        r3c9=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
        r9c6=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r5c9=r6c6], r9c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r9c6). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c6=r7c9]) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r7c9=r8c3], [r3c9=r9c6], r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  2.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r5c9=r6c6], r8c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c6, [r3c9=r8c6], [r5c9=r6c6], r7c9} (spine [r3c9=r8c6]â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r5c9=r6c6], [r3c6=r7c9], [r3c9=r8c6], r8c8} (spine [r3c6=r7c9]â€”[r3c9=r8c6]).
      â†’ color([r5c9=r6c6]) = color(r8c8). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r3c9=r8c6], [r3c6=r7c9], [r5c9=r6c6=r8c8], r9c7} (spine [r3c6=r7c9]â€”[r5c9=r6c6=r8c8]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r8c6]) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r8c6=r9c7], r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r5c9=r6c6], r8c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {[r3c9=r8c6]} = {r7c9}.
      â†’ color([r3c9=r8c6]) = color(r7c9), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:

  1.  SET: {row 6, row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 6, box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r6c6, r8c9} = {r5c9, r7c6}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, r7c6}:
      Diagonals: {r3c6, r5c9} and {r3c9, r7c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r5c9) = X

        row 6 {r6c6, r6c7, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r7c6) = X
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r3c6, r6c6â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r5c9, r9c9â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c4, r8c5}: r7c6=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c4, r8c5}: r8c4, r8c5â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r5c9=r6c6], r7c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c6, [r3c9=r7c6], [r5c9=r6c6], r9c9} (spine [r3c9=r7c6]â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r3c9=r7c6], r8c4, r8c5, r8c3} (spine r8c4â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r7c6]) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r9c9], [r3c9=r7c6=r8c3], r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r5c9=r6c6], r7c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r9c9} = {[r3c9=r7c6]}.
      â†’ color(r9c9) = color([r3c9=r7c6]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c5} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_ladder] (xwing+oddwheel+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r6c3, r8c7:
      Case A: color(r6c3) = color(r8c7). Identify.
        2.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c3=r8c7] forces rim to 2 colors.

  2.  Diamond {r3c9, [r6c9=r7c8], r7c9, r8c7} (spine [r6c9=r7c8]â€”r7c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c7). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {[r3c9=r8c7], r3c6, r8c6, r9c6} (spine r3c6â€”r8c6).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r8c7]) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r8c7=r9c6], r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {col 8, col 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 6, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r3c9} = {r8c7}.
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c7). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r9c1 guards oddagon {r9c1, r8c3, [r3c9=r8c7], r3c6, r9c6} (source r8c6).
      â†’ color(r9c1) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r8c6=r9c1] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, [r3c9=r8c7], r8c3, r9c2, r9c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_set] (diamond+xwing+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2, r9c6} (spine r9c1â€”r9c2).

        col 6 {r3c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r6c9, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        r3c6=X and r3c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r6c7, r6c9, r6c3} (spine r6c7â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r8c3, [r5c8=r6c3], r9c3, r9c8} (spine [r5c8=r6c3]â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r8c9, [r8c3=r9c8], r8c6, r9c6} (spine [r8c3=r9c8]â€”r8c6).
      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r8c9=r9c6], r8c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r8c9=r9c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {r3c6, [r3c9=r8c6], [r8c9=r9c6], r6c9} (spine [r3c9=r8c6]â€”[r8c9=r9c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {[r3c6=r6c9], [r3c9=r8c6], [r8c9=r9c6], [r8c3=r9c8]} (spine [r3c9=r8c6]â€”[r8c9=r9c6]).
      â†’ color([r3c6=r6c9]) = color([r8c3=r9c8]). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {r6c7, [r3c6=r6c9=r8c3=r9c8], [r5c8=r6c3], r9c3} (spine [r3c6=r6c9=r8c3=r9c8]â€”[r5c8=r6c3]).
      â†’ color(r6c7) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  8.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r6c9=r8c3=r9c8], [r6c7=r9c3], r7c7, [r8c9=r9c6]}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c9â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), r7c7 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Diamond {r6c9, r6c3, r6c7, r7c7} (spine r6c3â€”r6c7).
      â†’ color(r6c9) = color(r7c7). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c9=r7c7] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r8c9, r9c8, r5c8, r6c7, r6c3} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c9â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), r7c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r6c3, r8c3, r8c6}:
      Diagonals: {r3c6, r8c3} and {r6c3, r8c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r6c3, r9c3â‰ X

        col 8 {r5c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r5c8, r6c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r7c9, r8c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c3) = color(r8c6) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c7, r6c8}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c7, r6c8â‰ X

        r6c3=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X (adjacent)

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_ladder] (xwing+branch) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r5c9:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r5c9). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c6, r8c8, r9c8, r9c6}:

              r6c3=X and r6c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.

              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c6=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X

            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c8} and {r8c8, r9c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r8c3) = color(r9c8) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c3, r9c6â‰ X
              col 3 {r5c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r5c3â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              col 8 {r6c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r3c6=X â†’ r3c9, r6c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c2, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c2=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c8) = color(r9c3) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c3=X â†’ r9c6, r9c8â‰ X

              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_oddwheel] (diamond+ladder+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c8}:

      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r6c2). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c8, r9c8, r9c3}:
      Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c8} and {r8c8, r9c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

      Case 2: color(r8c8) = color(r9c3) = X

      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  SET: {row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 4, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r6c9, [r7c6=r8c9], r8c3} = {r5c3, [r7c6=r8c9], r9c5}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r7c6=r8c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c9, r8c3, r8c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        box 4 {[r5c3=r6c8], r6c1, r6c2}: [r5c3=r6c8]=X â†’ r6c1, r6c2â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c9, r8c9, r9c8}: r8c9=X â†’ r9c8â‰ X
        row 9 {[r8c6=r9c3], r9c6, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c9) = color([r8c6=r9c3]) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, [r8c6=r9c3], r8c9}: [r8c6=r9c3]=X â†’ r8c3, r8c9â‰ X
        row 9 {[r8c6=r9c3], r9c6, r9c8}: [r8c6=r9c3]=X â†’ r9c6, r9c8â‰ X

        col 6 {r3c6, [r8c6=r9c3], r9c6}: [r8c6=r9c3]=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r7c9, r8c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c9, r8c9, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c3, r6c1, r6c2, r6c8} (spine r6c1â€”r6c2).
      â†’ color(r5c3) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c9â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites [r5c3=r6c8] (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), r7c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub r7c9 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r8c9, r9c8, [r5c3=r6c8]} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        box 6 {r5c7, r5c8, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

      Case 2: color(r5c7) = color(r9c3) = X

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c3, r5c7, r5c8, r6c8} (spine r5c7â€”r5c8).
      â†’ color(r5c3) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c6, [r5c3=r6c8], r6c3, r9c3} (spine [r5c3=r6c8]â€”r6c3).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c6, [r6c6=r9c3], r9c6, r9c7} (spine [r6c6=r9c3]â€”r9c6).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r9c7], r3c9, r7c9, r8c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r9c7:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r9c7). Identify.
        2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r9c7], r3c9, r7c9, r8c9}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r9c7). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c9, r7c9, r9c7}:
            Diagonals: {r3c6, r7c9} and {r3c9, r9c7} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r7c9) = X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r6c6, r9c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r7c9, r8c9}: r7c9=X â†’ r3c9, r8c9â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c9, r9c7}: r7c9=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c6, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c3=X
              col 3 {r5c3, r6c3, r9c3}: r9c3=X â†’ r5c3, r6c3â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c3, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
              box 6 {r5c7, r5c8, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r5c7, r5c8â‰ X
              row 5 {r5c3, r5c7, r5c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r9c7) = X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c7=X â†’ r9c3, r9c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r7c9, r8c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r7c9, r8c9â‰ X
              r3c9=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c7=X â†’ r5c7â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r6c6=X
              r6c6=X â†’ r6c3, r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r5c3, r6c3, r9c3}: r6c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r5c3=X
              box 6 {r5c7, r5c8, r6c8}: r5c7, r6c8â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X
              r5c3=X and r5c8=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (ladder+xwing) ===

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r3c9, r5c6â€”r5c9, r9c6â€”r9c9}:
      Satellites r5c3 (rung 2), r9c2 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  2.  Circular ladder {r5c3â€”r9c2, r5c6â€”r9c6, r5c9â€”r9c9}:

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r7c8:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r7c8). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r7c8], r3c9, r4c9, r6c8}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c6=r7c8], r4c9} and {r3c9, r6c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c6=r7c8]) = color(r4c9) = X
              col 6 {[r3c6=r7c8], r6c6, r8c6}: [r3c6=r7c8]=X â†’ r6c6, r8c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r4c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X

              box 9 {[r3c6=r7c8], r8c7, r9c7}: [r3c6=r7c8]=X â†’ r8c7, r9c7â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c7}: r8c6, r8c7â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X

              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X

              r3c9=X â†’ [r3c6=r7c8]â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c8=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)

              box 9 {[r3c6=r7c8], r8c7, r9c7}: [r3c6=r7c8], r8c7â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
              r9c7=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r7c8). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r6c6, r6c8, r7c8}:
            Diagonals: {r3c6, r6c8} and {r6c6, r7c8} (non-adjacent).

        box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r8c5=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
        r6c8=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6, r7c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r4c9, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        r3c6=X and r3c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9, r6c8} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {[r3c9=r6c8], r3c6, r6c6, r7c6} (spine r3c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8]) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r8c8, [r3c9=r6c8=r7c6], r8c5, r9c4} (spine [r3c9=r6c8=r7c6]â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r8c8) = color(r9c4). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r8c5, [r3c9=r6c8=r7c6], [r8c8=r9c4], r9c8} (spine [r3c9=r6c8=r7c6]â€”[r8c8=r9c4]).
      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {r8c3, [r8c5=r9c8], [r8c8=r9c4], r9c2}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r8c8:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r8c8). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r8c8], r3c9, r4c9, r6c8}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c6=r8c8], r4c9} and {r3c9, r6c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c6=r8c8]) = color(r4c9) = X
              row 8 {[r3c6=r8c8], r8c3, r8c5}: [r3c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r8c3, r8c5â‰ X
              col 6 {[r3c6=r8c8], r6c6, r7c6}: [r3c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r6c6, r7c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r4c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r4c9=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c5}: r7c6, r8c5â‰ X â†’ r9c5=X
              r9c5=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
              r3c9=X â†’ [r3c6=r8c8]â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c8=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {[r3c6=r8c8], r6c6, r7c6}: [r3c6=r8c8], r6c6â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c5}: r7c6=X â†’ r8c5, r9c5â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c6=r8c8], r8c3, r8c5}: [r3c6=r8c8], r8c5â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r8c8). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r6c6, r6c8, r8c8}:
            Diagonals: {r3c6, r6c8} and {r6c6, r8c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r6c8) = X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r6c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
              r3c6=X â†’ r3c9, r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c6) = color(r8c8) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c3, r8c5â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c5}: r7c6, r8c5â‰ X â†’ r9c5=X
              r6c6=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c5=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              row 8 {[r3c6=r8c8], r8c3, r8c5}: [r3c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r8c3, r8c5â‰ X

              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X

      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r8c8). Add edge.

Proof of non-3-colorability:

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c7}:

  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r6c3, r8c3, r8c6}:
      Diagonals: {r3c6, r8c3} and {r6c3, r8c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r8c3) = X

        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r3c6, r6c6â‰ X
        r8c3=X â†’ r7c8, r8c7â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c3, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X

        box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9, r6c8} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {[r3c9=r6c8], r3c6, r6c6, r9c6} (spine r3c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8]) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r6c6, [r3c9=r6c8=r9c6], r6c3, r9c3} (spine [r3c9=r6c8=r9c6]â€”r6c3).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c6, [r3c9=r6c8=r9c6], [r6c6=r9c3], r6c3} (spine [r3c9=r6c8=r9c6]â€”[r6c6=r9c3]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r6c3). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r3c9=r6c8=r9c6], [r3c6=r6c3], [r6c6=r9c3], r8c3} (spine [r3c6=r6c3]â€”[r6c6=r9c3]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8=r9c6]) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r6c8=r8c3=r9c6], r7c8, r8c7, r9c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_ladder] (diamond+xwing+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9, r6c8} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r9c3 guards oddagon {r9c3, r6c3, [r3c9=r6c8], r3c6, r9c6} (source r6c6).
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r6c6=r9c3], r8c3, r8c7, r9c8}:
      Diagonals: {[r6c6=r9c3], r8c7} and {r8c3, r9c8} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r6c6=r9c3]) = color(r8c7) = X
        row 6 {[r3c9=r6c8], r6c3, [r6c6=r9c3]}: [r6c6=r9c3]=X â†’ [r3c9=r6c8], r6c3â‰ X
        row 9 {[r6c6=r9c3], r9c6, r9c8}: [r6c6=r9c3]=X â†’ r9c6, r9c8â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r6c6=r9c3], r8c3}: [r6c6=r9c3]=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r6c6=r9c3], r9c6}: [r6c6=r9c3]=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
        col 8 {[r3c9=r6c8], r7c8, r9c8}: [r3c9=r6c8], r9c8â‰ X â†’ r7c8=X
        box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c8}: r7c8=X and r8c7=X clash
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c3) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 9 {[r6c6=r9c3], r9c6, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ [r6c6=r9c3], r9c6â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r6c6=r9c3], r8c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r6c6=r9c3], r9c6}: [r6c6=r9c3], r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 8 {[r3c9=r6c8], r7c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ [r3c9=r6c8], r7c8â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c8, r8c7, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r8c7â‰ X
        row 6 {[r3c9=r6c8], r6c3, [r6c6=r9c3]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9, r6c8} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {[r3c9=r6c8]â€”r9c8, r6c3â€”r9c3, r6c6â€”r9c6}:
      Satellites r7c8 (rung 1), r8c3 (rung 2), r3c6 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub r7c8 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, [r3c9=r6c8], r9c8, r8c7, r8c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              row 9 {r9c2, r9c5, r9c8}: r9c5=X â†’ r9c2, r9c8â‰ X

              r9c5=X â†’ r8c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r8c6}: r6c6, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r4c9, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              r3c6=X and r3c9=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {col 8, col 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 6, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r3c9} = {r8c7}.
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r8c7). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r9c2 guards oddagon {r9c2, r8c3, [r3c9=r8c7], r3c6, r9c6} (source r8c6).
      â†’ color(r9c2) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r8c6=r9c2] forces rim to 2 colors.

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_ladder] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r4c9:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r4c9). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c6, r8c8, r9c8, r9c6}:
            Diagonals: {r8c6, r9c8} and {r8c8, r9c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r8c6) = color(r9c8) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c6=X â†’ r8c3, r8c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c3, r9c6â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 6 {[r3c6=r4c9], r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6=X â†’ [r3c6=r4c9]â‰ X
              col 8 {r5c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              box 6 {[r3c6=r4c9], r5c8, r6c7}: [r3c6=r4c9], r5c8â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r6c3=X and r6c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c8) = color(r9c6) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c6=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 6 {[r3c6=r4c9], r8c6, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ [r3c6=r4c9]â‰ X
              col 8 {r5c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              box 6 {[r3c6=r4c9], r5c8, r6c7}: [r3c6=r4c9], r5c8â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r6c3=X and r6c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r4c9). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c8, r9c8, r9c3}:
            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c8} and {r8c8, r9c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r8c3) = color(r9c8) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c3, r9c6â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              col 8 {r5c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r3c6=X â†’ r3c9, r4c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r7c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c8) = color(r9c3) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c6, r9c8}: r9c3=X â†’ r9c6, r9c8â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r9c3=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r8c6, r9c6}: r8c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              col 8 {r5c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              r3c6=X â†’ r3c9, r4c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r7c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_oddwheel] (diamond+ladder+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r8c3â€”r9c3, r8c6â€”r9c6, r8c8â€”r9c8}:
      Satellites r6c3 (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), r5c8 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Diamond {r3c6, r5c8, r6c3, r6c7} (spine r5c8â€”r6c3).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r6c7). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c8, r9c8, r9c3}:
      Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c8} and {r8c8, r9c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r5c3) = color(r6c9) = X
        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c6], r6c6, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ [r6c3=r9c6], r6c6â‰ X
        col 3 {r5c3, [r6c3=r9c6], r9c3}: r5c3=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r6c3=r9c6], r6c6}: [r6c3=r9c6], r6c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c7], r4c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ [r3c9=r9c7], r4c9â‰ X
        box 6 {r4c9, r5c8, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
        row 9 {[r3c9=r9c7], [r6c3=r9c6], r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r5c8) = color([r6c3=r9c6]) = X
        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c6], r6c6, r6c9}: [r6c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X
        row 9 {[r3c9=r9c7], [r6c3=r9c6], r9c3}: [r6c3=r9c6]=X â†’ [r3c9=r9c7], r9c3â‰ X
        col 3 {r5c3, [r6c3=r9c6], r9c3}: [r6c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r5c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r6c3=r9c6], r6c6}: [r6c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
        col 8 {r5c8, r7c8, r8c8}: r5c8=X â†’ r7c8, r8c8â‰ X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c7], r4c9, r6c9}: [r3c9=r9c7], r6c9â‰ X â†’ r4c9=X
        box 6 {r4c9, r5c8, r6c9}: r4c9=X and r5c8=X clash
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c8, r7c8, r8c8, r9c7} (spine r7c8â€”r8c8).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {[r5c8=r9c7]â€”r6c9, r9c3â€”r6c3, r9c6â€”r6c6}:
      Satellites r4c9 (rung 1), r5c3 (rung 2), r3c6 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub r4c9 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c9, [r5c8=r9c7], r5c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

      Diagonals: {r3c6, r6c3} and {r3c9, r6c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r6c3) = X

        box 9 {r7c8, r8c8, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

      Satellites r9c3 (rung 1), r3c6 (rung 2), r4c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub r4c9 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, [r6c8=r9c7], r9c3} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r6c8:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r6c8). Identify.
        2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r6c8], r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r6c8). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c9, r4c9, r6c8}:
            Diagonals: {r3c6, r4c9} and {r3c9, r6c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r4c9) = X
              col 6 {r3c6, r5c6, r9c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r5c6, r9c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r4c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r4c9=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X
              row 5 {r5c3, r5c6, r5c9}: r5c6, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r5c3=X
              col 3 {r5c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r5c3=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 4 {r5c3, r6c1, r6c2}: r5c3=X â†’ r6c1, r6c2â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3, r8c3â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              row 6 {r6c1, r6c2, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X
              row 6 {r6c1, r6c2, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c1, r6c2â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
              box 4 {r5c3, r6c1, r6c2}: r6c1, r6c2â‰ X â†’ r5c3=X
              r3c9=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r5c3=X â†’ r5c6, r7c3, r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {r3c6, r5c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r5c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3, r8c3â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              r9c2=X and r9c6=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r7c9, r8c7, r8c8, r8c3} (spine r8c7â€”r8c8).

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET+ladder) ===

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r6c6, r3c8â€”r6c8, r3c9â€”r6c9}:

      Remainder: {r9c6} = {r7c9}.
      â†’ color(r9c6) = color(r7c9), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c7} (spine r9c2â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c9, [r8c3=r9c7], r8c7, r8c6} (spine [r8c3=r9c7]â€”r8c7).
      â†’ color(r7c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r7c9=r8c6]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c9=r8c6]). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r7c9=r8c6], r6c6, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET+ladder) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r6c6, r3c8â€”r6c8, r3c9â€”r6c9}:
      Satellites r8c6 (rung 1), r7c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  2.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r8c6} = {r7c9}.
      â†’ color(r8c6) = color(r7c9), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r7c6, r9c4, r9c5, r9c2} (spine r9c4â€”r9c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r9c2). Identify.

      Remainder: {r8c3} = {[r7c6=r9c2]}.
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color([r7c6=r9c2]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 3, row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 6, col 8, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r8c3, r8c5} = {r7c6, r9c8}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Diamond {r8c5, r7c6, r9c5, r9c8} (spine r7c6â€”r9c5).
      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r8c9, r8c3, [r8c5=r9c8], r9c2} (spine r8c3â€”[r8c5=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r8c3, [r8c5=r9c8], [r8c9=r9c2], r9c5} (spine [r8c5=r9c8]â€”[r8c9=r9c2]).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {r7c6, [r8c3=r9c5], [r8c5=r9c8], [r8c9=r9c2]} (spine [r8c3=r9c5]â€”[r8c5=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color([r8c9=r9c2]). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r7c6=r8c9=r9c2]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c6=r8c9=r9c2]). Identify.
  7.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r7c6=r8c9=r9c2], r6c6, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r6c6, r3c8â€”r6c8, r3c9â€”r6c9}:
      Satellites r7c6 (rung 1), r9c8 (rung 2), r8c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  2.  Diamond {r8c9, r7c6, r8c5, r9c5} (spine r7c6â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r8c9=r9c5] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r7c6, r8c5, r8c3, r9c2, r9c8} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c8} (spine r9c2â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  2.  SET: {row 3, row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 6, col 8, col 9}.
      Remainder: {[r8c3=r9c8], r8c5} = {r7c6, [r8c3=r9c8]}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Diamond {r7c6, [r8c3=r9c8], r8c5, r8c9} (spine [r8c3=r9c8]â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c9). Identify.

      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c6=r8c9]). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r7c6=r8c9], r6c6, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r7c6=r8c9], r6c6, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_ladder] (xwing+branch) ===

  1.  Branch on r6c3, r8c8:
      Case A: color(r6c3) = color(r8c8). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c8, [r6c3=r8c8], r8c6}:

              r9c3=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r6c3=r8c8], r6c8, r6c9}: [r6c3=r8c8], r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c8â€”r3c9, r6c8â€”r6c9, r8c8â€”r7c9}:

  2.  Diamond {r8c3, r6c3, r9c3, r9c7} (spine r6c3â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c7). Identify.

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_ladder] (diamond+xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r8c3, r9c5:
      Case A: color(r8c3) = color(r9c5). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r3c9, r6c9, r6c8}:
            Diagonals: {r3c8, r6c9} and {r3c9, r6c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c8) = color(r6c9) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X

              r9c7=X â†’ [r8c3=r9c5], r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r6c3, [r8c3=r9c5], r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c9â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r7c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c8, r7c9, r9c7}: r7c8, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
              r9c7=X â†’ [r8c3=r9c5], r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r6c3, [r8c3=r9c5], r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r8c3) â‰  color(r9c5). Add edge.
        3.  Diamond {r8c6, r8c3, r9c5, r9c3} (spine r8c3â€”r9c5).
            â†’ color(r8c6) = color(r9c3). Identify.

            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

=== Greedy proof (diamond+parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c6, r7c6, r8c6, r9c5} (spine r7c6â€”r8c6).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, [r3c6=r9c5], r3c8, r9c8} (spine [r3c6=r9c5]â€”r3c8).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c8, [r3c6=r9c5], [r3c9=r9c8], r9c2} (spine [r3c6=r9c5]â€”[r3c9=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {[r3c6=r9c5], [r3c8=r9c2], [r3c9=r9c8], r6c8} (spine [r3c8=r9c2]â€”[r3c9=r9c8]).
      â†’ color([r3c6=r9c5]) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  5.  Parity transport:
      row 3 {[r3c6=r6c8=r9c5], [r3c8=r9c2], [r3c9=r9c8]}
      row 6 {r6c3, [r3c6=r6c8=r9c5], r6c9}
      row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}
      row 9 {[r3c8=r9c2], [r3c6=r6c8=r9c5], [r3c9=r9c8]}
      Parallel links: row 3â†”row 6 (box, col, col); row 6â†”row 8 (col, box, col); row 8â†”row 9 (box, box, box)
      â†’ same permutation parity.
      Each pair shares a column â†’ distinct permutations.
      4 same-parity permutations from 3 available â†’ pigeonhole contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r8c9:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r8c9). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r8c9], r6c9, r6c3, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c6=r8c9], r6c3} and {r6c9, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c6=r8c9]) = color(r6c3) = X
              row 3 {[r3c6=r8c9], r3c8, r3c9}: [r3c6=r8c9]=X â†’ r3c8, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c8, r6c9â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c6=r8c9], r8c3, r8c6}: [r3c6=r8c9]=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              col 6 {[r3c6=r8c9], r7c6, r8c6}: [r3c6=r8c9]=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r3c8, r6c8â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c6, r9c5}: r7c6, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r9c5=X
              r9c5=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              [r3c6=r8c9]=X and r9c8=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c8â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c6=r8c9], r8c3, r8c6}: r8c3=X â†’ [r3c6=r8c9], r8c6â‰ X
              col 6 {[r3c6=r8c9], r7c6, r8c6}: [r3c6=r8c9], r8c6â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
              col 9 {[r3c6=r8c9], r3c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c6, r9c5}: r7c6=X â†’ r9c5â‰ X
              r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {[r3c6=r8c9], r3c8, r3c9}: [r3c6=r8c9], r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c8=X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c5, r9c8}: r9c2, r9c5â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r3c8=X and r9c8=X clash
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r8c9). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c8, r6c9, r8c9, r9c8}:
            Diagonals: {r6c8, r8c9} and {r6c9, r9c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r6c8) = color(r8c9) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c9â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r3c8, r9c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X
              r8c9=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r9c8) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c5, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c2, r9c5â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X

              box 8 {r7c6, r8c6, r9c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 6, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 3, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r6c8, r9c2} = {r3c9, r8c3}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c8, r6c9, r9c9, r9c2}:
      Diagonals: {r6c8, r9c9} and {r6c9, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c8) = color(r9c9) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c9â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X

        r8c3=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X (adjacent)

        r3c6=X and r7c6=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_set] (diamond+oddwheel) ===

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r8c3, r9c3, r9c2} (spine r8c3â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.

      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r6c3=r9c2] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c8, r6c8, r6c9, r9c9, r9c3, [r7c6=r8c3]} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r6c3:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r6c3). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r6c3], r7c6, r8c4, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c6=r6c3], r8c4} and {r7c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c6=r6c3]) = color(r8c4) = X
              row 3 {[r3c6=r6c3], r3c8, r3c9}: [r3c6=r6c3]=X â†’ r3c8, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {[r3c6=r6c3], r6c8, r6c9}: [r3c6=r6c3]=X â†’ r6c8, r6c9â‰ X

              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r3c9, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c4, r8c5}: r8c4=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
              r9c9=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c4, r8c5}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
              r7c6=X â†’ [r3c6=r6c3]â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c9}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r3c9, r6c9â‰ X
              row 3 {[r3c6=r6c3], r3c8, r3c9}: [r3c6=r6c3], r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c8=X
              row 6 {[r3c6=r6c3], r6c8, r6c9}: [r3c6=r6c3], r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
              r3c8=X and r6c8=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r6c3). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c8, r6c8, r6c3}:
            Diagonals: {r3c6, r6c8} and {r3c8, r6c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r6c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6=X â†’ r3c8, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c9â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r3c9, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X
              r3c6=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c9=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              r8c3=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c4, r8c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r6c9, r8c9, r8c3}:
      Diagonals: {r6c3, r8c9} and {r6c9, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c3) = color(r8c9) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r8c3, r8c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c8, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r7c8, r8c8}: r7c8, r8c8â‰ X â†’ r3c8=X
        r3c8=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
        r9c6=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c8, r8c9â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c8, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c8, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r7c8=X
        r7c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c6} = {r7c8}.
      â†’ color(r9c6) = color(r7c8). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r7c8=r9c6], r8c7, r8c3, r9c2}:
      Diagonals: {[r7c8=r9c6], r8c3} and {r8c7, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r7c8=r9c6]) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c7, r8c9}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c7, r8c9â‰ X
        row 9 {[r7c8=r9c6], r9c2, r9c3}: [r7c8=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X

        r3c6=X and r3c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c6} (spine r9c2â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r3c9, r6c6â€”r6c9, [r8c3=r9c6]â€”r8c9}:
      Satellites r3c8 (rung 1), r6c3 (rung 2), r8c7 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub r8c7 forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c8, r6c3, [r8c3=r9c6], r8c9, r7c8} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        r3c9=X and r6c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c7) = color(r9c2) = X

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r9c6], r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r6c3, r9c6:
      Case A: color(r6c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
        2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c3=r9c6], r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r6c3) â‰  color(r9c6). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c3, r8c3, r9c1, r9c6}:
            Diagonals: {r6c3, r9c1} and {r8c3, r9c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r6c3) = color(r9c1) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c6}: r9c1=X â†’ r9c2, r9c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r6c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r9c1=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X
              r3c6=X â†’ r3c8, r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r3c9, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r7c9=X
              box 9 {r7c8, r7c9, r8c8}: r7c9=X â†’ r7c8, r8c8â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r7c8, r8c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c3) = color(r9c6) = X
              row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r3c6, r6c6â‰ X
              r8c3=X â†’ r6c3, r8c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c3, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r3c9, r7c9â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c8, r7c9, r8c8}: r7c9, r8c8â‰ X â†’ r7c8=X
              r7c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              row 9 {r9c2, r9c8, r9c9}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c2, r9c9â‰ X

              r7c6=X â†’ r3c6, r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c3=X â†’ [r3c8=r6c3]â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c3], r3c9}: r3c6, [r3c8=r6c3]â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X

      Case B: color(r3c8) â‰  color(r6c3). Add edge.

            Diagonals: {r3c8, r6c9} and {r3c9, r6c3} (non-adjacent).

      â†’ color(r6c3) = color([r8c3=r9c8]), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r7c6, r7c7, r7c9, r9c8} (spine r7c7â€”r7c9).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r9c8). Identify.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_ladder] (SET+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 7, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r7c6} = {r8c3}.
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r9c8, r9c2}:
      Diagonals: {[r7c6=r8c3], r9c8} and {r7c7, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r7c6=r8c3]) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r7c9}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r7c7, r7c9â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r7c6=r8c3], r9c3}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]}: [r7c6=r8c3]=X â†’ r3c6, r6c6â‰ X
        r9c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c8â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c3, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r3c9=X and r6c9=X clash
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r7c7) = color(r9c2) = X
        row 7 {[r7c6=r8c3], r7c7, r7c9}: r7c7=X â†’ [r7c6=r8c3], r7c9â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c8}: r9c2=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
        col 3 {r6c3, [r7c6=r8c3], r9c3}: [r7c6=r8c3], r9c3â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
        r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]}: r6c6, [r7c6=r8c3]â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r6c9, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        r3c6=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        r3c6=X and r3c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        [r7c3=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X (adjacent)
        r6c3=X and [r7c3=r9c6]=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.

      â†’ color(r9c6) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c9, r6c3, r6c6, [r7c3=r9c6]} (spine r6c3â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c9) = color([r7c3=r9c6]). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c8, r9c8, [r6c9=r7c3=r9c6]}:
      Diagonals: {r3c6, r9c8} and {r3c8, [r6c9=r7c3=r9c6]} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r9c8) = X

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c6} = {r7c3}.
      â†’ color(r9c6) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  2.  SET: {row 3, row 6}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 6, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r3c8, r6c3} = {[r7c3=r9c6], r8c9}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c8, r6c3, [r7c3=r9c6]}:

        r8c9=X â†’ r8c2â‰ X (adjacent)

        r8c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X (adjacent)

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c2, r8c8, r8c9, r9c8} (spine r8c8â€”r8c9).
      â†’ color(r8c2) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  2.  SET: {row 3, row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 6, col 8, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r6c3, [r8c2=r9c8]} = {[r8c2=r9c8], r9c6}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Guardian: r6c6 guards oddagon {r6c6, r6c3, r7c3, r9c1, r9c6} (source [r8c2=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color([r8c2=r9c8]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c6=r8c2=r9c8] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c8, r8c8, r8c9, r6c9, r6c3, r7c3, r9c1, r9c6} (length 9) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+parity) ===

          r6â†”r7: r6c3â†”r7c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 3, row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 3, col 6, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r3c8, r8c5} = {r7c3, r9c6}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Guardian: r6c6 guards oddagon {r6c6, r6c3, r8c3, r9c2, r9c6} (source r7c3).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  3.  Guardian: r3c9 guards oddagon {r3c9, r3c6, r9c6, r9c2, r8c3, r6c3, r6c9} (source [r6c6=r7c3]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color([r6c6=r7c3]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r6c6=r7c3] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c8, r7c8, r8c9, r8c3, r9c2, r9c6} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r9c8} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r9c8) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r7c6=r9c8], r8c5, r8c3, r9c2}:
      Diagonals: {[r7c6=r9c8], r8c3} and {r8c5, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r7c6=r9c8]) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c6}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c5, r8c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r7c6=r9c8], r9c2, r9c3}: [r7c6=r9c8]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r7c6=r9c8], r8c6}: [r7c6=r9c8]=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, [r7c6=r9c8]}: [r7c6=r9c8]=X â†’ r3c8, r6c8â‰ X
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c8â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X

        box 6 {r5c9, r6c8, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c5) = color(r9c2) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c6}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r7c6=r9c8], r9c2, r9c3}: r9c2=X â†’ [r7c6=r9c8], r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r7c6=r9c8], r8c6}: [r7c6=r9c8], r8c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X

        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, [r7c6=r9c8]}: r3c8, [r7c6=r9c8]â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
        box 6 {r5c9, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8=X â†’ r5c9, r6c9â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r5c9, r6c9, r6c8} (spine r5c9â€”r6c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c6, r3c8, [r3c9=r6c8], r9c8} (spine r3c8â€”[r3c9=r6c8]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {[r3c6=r9c8]} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color([r3c6=r9c8]) = color(r7c6), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (SET+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r9c8} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r9c8) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r5c9 guards oddagon {r5c9, r3c9, r3c6, [r7c6=r9c8], r6c8} (source r3c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r3c8). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c8=r5c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c9, r6c8, [r7c6=r9c8]} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c8â€”r3c9, r6c8â€”r5c9, r8c8â€”r7c9}:
      Satellites r3c6 (rung 1), r8c7 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, r3c9, r7c9, r8c7}:
      Diagonals: {r3c6, r7c9} and {r3c9, r8c7} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r7c9) = X
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6=X â†’ r3c8, r3c9â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r6c6, r9c6â‰ X

        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c6=X â†’ r8c3, r8c8â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r8c6}: r8c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r8c8}: r3c8, r8c8â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r5c9, r7c9â‰ X
        box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c7}: r7c9, r8c8â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
        r9c7=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2, r9c7} (spine r9c1â€”r9c2).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c9, [r8c3=r9c7], r8c8, r8c6} (spine [r8c3=r9c7]â€”r8c8).
      â†’ color(r7c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r7c9=r8c6]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c9=r8c6]). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r3c8=r7c9=r8c6], r6c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r3c8=r7c9=r8c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r7c9=r8c6], [r3c9=r6c6], r5c9, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_ladder] (diamond+SET+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r8c6} = {r7c9}.
      â†’ color(r8c6) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r7c9=r8c6]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c9=r8c6]). Identify.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c8=r7c9=r8c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, r6c8, r6c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  2.  Diamond {r7c6, [r8c3=r9c5], r8c5, r8c9} (spine [r8c3=r9c5]â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r7c6=r8c9]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c6=r8c9]). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r3c8=r7c6=r8c9], r6c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r3c8=r7c6=r8c9]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r7c6=r8c9], [r3c9=r6c6], r5c9, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r8c9} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r8c9) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r6c8 guards oddagon {r6c8, r3c8, r3c9, [r7c6=r8c9], r6c6} (source r3c6).
      â†’ color(r6c8) = color(r3c6). Identify.
  3.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r6c8], r3c9, r5c9, [r7c6=r8c9]}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_set] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c5} (spine r9c2â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c6, [r8c3=r9c5], r8c5, r8c9} (spine [r8c3=r9c5]â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c9). Identify.

      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c6=r8c9]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c8=r7c6=r8c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, r6c8, r6c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_set] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r7c6, r8c4, r8c5, r8c3} (spine r8c4â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {[r7c6=r8c3], r9c1, r9c2, r9c9} (spine r9c1â€”r9c2).
      â†’ color([r7c6=r8c3]) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r7c6=r8c3=r9c9]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r7c6=r8c3=r9c9]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c8=r7c6=r8c3=r9c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, r6c8, r6c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        r6c6=X and r6c7=X are adjacent

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r8c6=r9c8], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

        col 6 {r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r6c6]}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c6]â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r6c6], r7c9}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
        box 6 {[r5c9=r6c6], r6c7, r6c8}: [r5c9=r6c6], r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
        r8c3=X and r8c8=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r5c9=r6c6]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r5c9=r6c6]). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2, r9c6} (spine r9c1â€”r9c2).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c8=r5c9=r6c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r7c9, r8c8, [r8c3=r9c6]} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡
