  2.  Diamond {r6c7, r3c6, r6c6, r9c6} (spine r3c6â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c7) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c9, r5c8, [r6c7=r9c6], r9c8} (spine r5c8â€”[r6c7=r9c6]).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9, [r5c9=r9c8]} (spine r3c8â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color([r5c9=r9c8]). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {r3c9, [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8], r3c8, r5c8} (spine [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8]â€”r3c8).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r5c8). Identify.
  6.  Diamond {r3c8, [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8], [r3c9=r5c8], [r6c7=r9c6]} (spine [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8]â€”[r3c9=r5c8]).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r6c7=r9c6]). Identify.
  7.  Diamond {[r3c9=r5c8], [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8], [r3c8=r6c7=r9c6], r6c6} (spine [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8]â€”[r3c8=r6c7=r9c6]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r5c8]) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  8.  Diamond {[r3c9=r5c8=r6c6], [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8], [r3c8=r6c7=r9c6], r9c2} (spine [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8]â€”[r3c8=r6c7=r9c6]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r5c8=r6c6]) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  9.  Diamond {[r3c8=r6c7=r9c6], [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8], [r3c9=r5c8=r6c6=r9c2], r8c9} (spine [r3c6=r5c9=r9c8]â€”[r3c9=r5c8=r6c6=r9c2]).
      â†’ color([r3c8=r6c7=r9c6]) = color(r8c9). Identify.
  10.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c8=r6c7=r8c9=r9c6], [r3c9=r5c8=r6c6=r9c2], r6c3, r8c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/4 [no_ladder] (hexagon+xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r8c9, r9c2:
      Case A: color(r8c9) = color(r9c2). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r3c9, [r8c9=r9c2], r9c8}:
            Diagonals: {r3c8, [r8c9=r9c2]} and {r3c9, r9c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c8) = color([r8c9=r9c2]) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 9 {[r8c9=r9c2], r9c6, r9c8}: [r8c9=r9c2]=X â†’ r9c6, r9c8â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r6c6=X
              col 8 {r3c8, r5c8, r9c8}: r3c8=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, [r8c9=r9c2]}: [r8c9=r9c2]=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r5c8, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
              r6c6=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              [r8c9=r9c2]=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c6=X and r6c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r9c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
              row 9 {[r8c9=r9c2], r9c6, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ [r8c9=r9c2], r9c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r9c6â‰ X â†’ r6c6=X
              col 8 {r3c8, r5c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, [r8c9=r9c2]}: r3c9=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r5c8, r5c9â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
              r6c6=X â†’ r6c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c6=X and r6c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r8c9) â‰  color(r9c2). Add edge.
        3.  Bridged hexagon: ring {r5c9, r6c7, r6c6, r9c6, r9c2, r8c9}
            Bridges: r5c8â€”r9c8 (edges r5c9â€”r6c7 â†” r9c6â€”r9c2), r6c3â€”r8c3 (edges r6c7â€”r6c6 â†” r9c2â€”r8c9), r3c6â€”r3c9 (edges r6c6â€”r9c6 â†” r8c9â€”r5c9).
            Each bridge forces opposite edges to miss different colors,
            but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/4 [no_diamond] (ladder+xwing+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c8â€”r3c9, r5c8â€”r5c9, r9c8â€”r8c9}:
      Satellites r3c6 (rung 1), r6c7 (rung 2) forced to distinct colors. Add edge.
  2.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r6c7, r3c8â€”r5c8, r3c9â€”r5c9}:
      Satellites r6c6 (rung 1), r9c8 (rung 2), r8c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Guardian: r5c8 guards oddagon {r5c8, r6c7, r3c6, r9c6, r9c8} (source r6c6).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r5c8=r6c6], r6c3, r8c3, r8c9}:
      Diagonals: {[r5c8=r6c6], r8c3} and {r6c3, r8c9} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r5c8=r6c6]) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 6 {[r5c8=r6c6], r6c3, r6c7}: [r5c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r5c8=r6c6], r9c6}: [r5c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r3c6, r9c6â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, [r5c8=r6c6], r9c8}: [r5c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r3c8, r9c8â‰ X
        box 6 {[r5c8=r6c6], r5c9, r6c7}: [r5c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
        [r5c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r8c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c8â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r6c3) = color(r8c9) = X
        row 6 {[r5c8=r6c6], r6c3, r6c7}: r6c3=X â†’ [r5c8=r6c6], r6c7â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X
        box 6 {[r5c8=r6c6], r5c9, r6c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/4 [no_xwing] (diamond+ladder+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Circular ladder {r3c8â€”r3c9, r5c8â€”r5c9, r9c8â€”r8c9}:

  3.  Diamond {r6c6, r9c6, r9c8, r9c2} (spine r9c6â€”r9c8).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c6=r9c2] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r6c7, r6c3, r8c3, r8c9, r9c8, r9c6} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c8) = color([r6c7=r9c6]) = X
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
        row 6 {[r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, [r6c7=r9c6]}: [r6c7=r9c6]=X â†’ [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], [r6c7=r9c6], r9c9}: [r6c7=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r3c9, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, [r6c7=r9c6]}: r5c9=X and [r6c7=r9c6]=X clash
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c7, [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, r9c6} (spine [r6c3=r9c2]â€”r6c6).
      â†’ color(r6c7) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c8, r5c9, [r6c7=r9c6], r9c9} (spine r5c9â€”[r6c7=r9c6]).
      â†’ color(r5c8) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9, [r5c8=r9c9]} (spine r3c8â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color([r5c8=r9c9]). Identify.
  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r5c8=r9c9], [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6, [r6c7=r9c6]}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c8, r6c6:
      Case A: color(r3c8) = color(r6c6). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c8=r6c6], r3c9, r5c9, r5c8}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c8=r6c6], r5c9} and {r3c9, r5c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c8=r6c6]) = color(r5c9) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r3c9}: [r3c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {[r3c8=r6c6], r6c3, r6c7}: [r3c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r9c6}: [r3c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r9c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r5c9=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c9}: r9c6, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r5c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6]â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r9c6}: r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6]â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r5c9, r9c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r5c8=X â†’ r6c7â‰ X
              r9c6=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r3c8=r6c6], r6c3, r6c7}: [r3c8=r6c6], r6c7â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c8) â‰  color(r6c6). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r5c8, r6c7, r6c6}:
            Diagonals: {r3c8, r6c7} and {r5c8, r6c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c8) = color(r6c7) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r5c8, r5c9â‰ X
              r9c6=X â†’ r9c2, r9c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r5c8) = color(r6c6) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c6=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6, r9c6â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r5c9, r6c7}: r5c8=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X
              r5c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c8â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c9}: r9c6, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).

      â†’ color(r6c7) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9, [r6c7=r9c6]} (spine r5c9â€”r9c9).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color([r6c7=r9c6]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r6c7=r9c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c8, r5c8, r5c9, r9c9, [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3, r9c8} (spine r9c2â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  2.  Circular ladder {r3c6â€”r8c6, r3c8â€”[r8c3=r9c8], r3c9â€”r8c9}:
      Satellites r5c6 (rung 1), r6c8 (rung 2), r6c9 (rung 3) forced to distinct colors. Add triangle.
  3.  Kâ‚„ on {r5c6, r6c5, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r3c9, r8c9, r9c8}:
      Diagonals: {r3c8, r8c9} and {r3c9, r9c8} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c8) = color(r8c9) = X
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r5c6, r8c6}: r3c6, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r3c8=X â†’ r6c8, r9c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r8c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
        r5c6=X â†’ r6c5â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c5, r6c8, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r9c8) = X
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r8c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r6c9, r8c9â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
        r8c3=X â†’ r8c6â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {r6c5, r6c8, r6c9}: r6c8, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c5=X
        col 6 {r3c6, r5c6, r8c6}: r3c6, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r5c6=X
        r5c6=X and r6c5=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+SET) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2, r9c8} (spine r9c1â€”r9c2).
      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  2.  SET: {row 3, row 6, row 8}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 6, col 8, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r6c5, [r8c3=r9c8]} = {r5c6, [r8c3=r9c8]}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Diamond {r6c5, r5c6, [r8c3=r9c8], r8c6} (spine r5c6â€”[r8c3=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r8c6). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c6, r5c6, [r6c5=r8c6], [r8c3=r9c8]} (spine r5c6â€”[r6c5=r8c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color([r8c3=r9c8]). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {r3c9, [r3c6=r8c3=r9c8], r3c8, r6c8} (spine [r3c6=r8c3=r9c8]â€”r3c8).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r8c3=r9c8], [r3c9=r6c8], [r6c5=r8c6], r8c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.

        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, [r3c9=r9c6]}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6]â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6], [r5c6=r6c9]}: r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6]â‰ X â†’ [r5c6=r6c9]=X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c6], [r5c6=r6c9], r7c9}: [r5c6=r6c9]=X and r7c9=X clash
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color([r3c9=r9c6]) = color(r8c8) = X
        row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, [r3c9=r9c6]}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
        row 9 {[r3c9=r9c6], r9c2, r9c3}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6], [r5c6=r6c9]}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ [r5c6=r6c9]â‰ X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c6], [r5c6=r6c9], r7c9}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r9c2, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
        r8c3=X and r8c8=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r6c4, r6c5, r6c9} (spine r6c4â€”r6c5).
      â†’ color(r5c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c8, r3c6, r3c9, [r5c6=r6c9]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color([r5c6=r6c9]). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9], r9c6} (spine r3c6â€”[r3c8=r5c6=r6c9]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Diamond {r3c6, [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9], [r3c9=r9c6], r7c9} (spine [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9]â€”[r3c9=r9c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r3c9=r9c6], [r3c6=r7c9], [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9], r8c8} (spine [r3c6=r7c9]â€”[r3c8=r5c6=r6c9]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r9c6]) = color(r8c8). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r8c8=r9c6], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===

  1.  Diamond {r5c6, r6c4, r6c5, r6c9} (spine r6c4â€”r6c5).

      â†’ color(r8c3) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r7c9, r8c8, [r8c3=r9c6]} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 6, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 5, box 7}.
      Remainder: {r6c9, r9c6} = {r5c6, r8c3}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Branch on r3c6, r6c9:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
        3.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r6c9], r5c6, r6c4, r6c5}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r6c9). Add edge.
        4.  Guardian: r8c8 guards oddagon {r8c8, r3c8, r3c6, r6c9, r7c9} (source r3c9).
            â†’ color(r8c8) = color(r3c9). Identify.
        5.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c6, [r3c9=r8c8], r8c3, r5c6}:
            Diagonals: {r3c6, r8c3} and {[r3c9=r8c8], r5c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, [r3c9=r8c8]}: r3c6=X â†’ r3c8, [r3c9=r8c8]â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r5c6, r9c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r5c6, r9c6â‰ X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
              r3c6=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color([r3c9=r8c8]) = color(r5c6) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, [r3c9=r8c8]}: [r3c9=r8c8]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r5c6, r9c6}: r5c6=X â†’ r9c6â‰ X
              col 9 {[r3c9=r8c8], r6c9, r7c9}: [r3c9=r8c8]=X â†’ r6c9, r7c9â‰ X
              box 5 {r5c6, r6c4, r6c5}: r5c6=X â†’ r6c4, r6c5â‰ X
              [r3c9=r8c8]=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c4, r6c5, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r7c9, [r8c3=r9c8], r8c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+oddwheel+branch+guardian) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r7c9, r8c6:
      Case A: color(r7c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
        2.  Guardian: r6c4 guards oddagon {r6c4, r5c6, r3c6, r3c9, r6c9} (source [r7c9=r8c6]).
            â†’ color(r6c4) = color([r7c9=r8c6]). Identify.

            Diagonals: {r3c8, r7c9} and {r3c9, r9c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r7c9}: r7c9=X â†’ r6c9â‰ X
              r3c8=X â†’ r9c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              r7c9=X â†’ r8c6â‰ X (adjacent)

              Contradiction.

              row 6 {[r3c6=r6c5], r6c3, r6c8}: [r3c6=r6c5], r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r6c5). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r5c9, r6c8, r6c5}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r6c8} and {r5c9, r6c5} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r6c8) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c5, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c5â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r3c8, r9c8â‰ X
              r5c6=X â†’ r3c6, r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c8â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c8, r9c9}: r9c8, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r5c9) = color(r6c5) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c5, r6c8}: r6c5=X â†’ r6c3, r6c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r9c9}: r5c9=X â†’ r3c9, r9c9â‰ X
              r5c9=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c5=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c8=X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r3c8=X â†’ r9c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c8, r9c9}: r9c8, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c5, [r6c3=r9c2], r6c8, r9c8} (spine [r6c3=r9c2]â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Guardian: r3c9 guards oddagon {r3c9, r3c8, r6c8, [r6c3=r9c2], r9c9} (source [r6c5=r9c8]).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color([r6c5=r9c8]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r6c5=r9c8] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c8, r6c8, r5c9, r5c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET) ===

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              r5c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              r3c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c6, r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c8) = color(r9c2) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c5, r9c8}: r9c2=X â†’ r9c5, r9c8â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              r6c8=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r6c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r6c6, [r6c3=r9c2], r6c8, r9c8} (spine [r6c3=r9c2]â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r6c6) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c9, r3c6, r3c8, [r6c6=r9c8]} (spine r3c6â€”r3c8).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color([r6c6=r9c8]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r6c6=r9c8] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r5c5, r5c9, r6c8, [r6c3=r9c2], r9c5} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c8, r6c3:
      Case A: color(r3c8) = color(r6c3). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c8=r6c3], r5c8, r5c5, r6c6}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c8=r6c3], r5c5} and {r5c8, r6c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c8=r6c3]) = color(r5c5) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c3], r3c9}: [r3c8=r6c3]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X

              [r3c8=r6c3]=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c2=X and r9c9=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r5c8) = color(r6c6) = X
              row 5 {r5c5, r5c8, r5c9}: r5c8=X â†’ r5c5, r5c9â‰ X
              col 8 {[r3c8=r6c3], r5c8, r9c8}: r5c8=X â†’ [r3c8=r6c3], r9c8â‰ X
              r6c6=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c3], r3c9}: r3c6, [r3c8=r6c3]â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_ladder] (hexagon+xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c9, r6c2:
      Case A: color(r5c9) = color(r6c2). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r3c9, [r5c9=r6c2], r6c8}:

            Case 1: color(r3c8) = color([r5c9=r6c2]) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X

              col 9 {r3c9, [r5c9=r6c2], r7c9}: [r5c9=r6c2]=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c7}: r7c9, r8c8â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
              [r5c9=r6c2]=X â†’ r5c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c6=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c6=X and r9c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X
              row 6 {[r5c9=r6c2], r6c6, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c2], r6c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, r8c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, [r5c9=r6c2], r7c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X

              r9c6=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c6=X and r9c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c9) â‰  color(r6c2). Add edge.
        3.  Bridged hexagon: ring {r5c9, r6c2, r6c6, r9c6, r9c7, r7c9}
            Bridges: r5c3â€”r9c3 (edges r5c9â€”r6c2 â†” r9c6â€”r9c7), r6c8â€”r8c8 (edges r6c2â€”r6c6 â†” r9c7â€”r7c9), r3c6â€”r3c9 (edges r6c6â€”r9c6 â†” r7c9â€”r5c9).
            Each bridge forces opposite edges to miss different colors,
            but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color(r5c3) = color(r6c8). Identify.

      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r6c6, [r5c3=r6c8], r9c3, r9c7} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {r8c8} = {r7c6}.
      â†’ color(r8c8) = color(r7c6). Identify.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r7c6=r8c8], r8c3, r9c2, r9c5}:
      Diagonals: {[r7c6=r8c8], r9c2} and {r8c3, r9c5} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r7c6=r8c8]) = color(r9c2) = X
        row 8 {[r7c6=r8c8], r8c3, r8c6}: [r7c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c5}: r9c2=X â†’ r9c3, r9c5â‰ X

        col 8 {r3c8, r6c8, [r7c6=r8c8]}: [r7c6=r8c8]=X â†’ r3c8, r6c8â‰ X

        row 9 {[r7c6=r9c8], r9c2, r9c3}: [r7c6=r9c8]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X

        box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c5) = color(r9c2) = X
        row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c6}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r7c6=r9c8], r9c2, r9c3}: r9c2=X â†’ [r7c6=r9c8], r9c3â‰ X

        box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

        row 9 {[r6c3=r9c2], [r6c7=r9c6], r9c9}: [r6c3=r9c2]=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X

        col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r9c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

      Case 2: color(r3c8) = color([r6c7=r9c6]) = X

        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c8, r6c6:
      Case A: color(r3c8) = color(r6c6). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c8=r6c6], r3c9, r4c9, r5c8}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c8=r6c6], r4c9} and {r3c9, r5c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c8=r6c6]) = color(r4c9) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r3c9}: [r3c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {[r3c8=r6c6], r6c3, r6c7}: [r3c8=r6c6]=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X

              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r9c9}: r4c9=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r4c9, r5c8, r6c7}: r4c9=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c9}: r9c6, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c9) = color(r5c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6]â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6], r9c6}: r3c6, [r3c8=r6c6]â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r4c9, r9c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r4c9, r5c8, r6c7}: r5c8=X â†’ r6c7â‰ X
              r9c6=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {[r3c8=r6c6], r6c3, r6c7}: [r3c8=r6c6], r6c7â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X
              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r6c3=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c8) â‰  color(r6c6). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c8, r5c8, r6c7, r6c6}:
            Diagonals: {r3c8, r6c7} and {r5c8, r6c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c8) = color(r6c7) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c7=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X

              Contradiction.

              row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c7}: r6c6=X â†’ r6c3, r6c7â‰ X

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===

      â†’ color(r3c9) = color([r6c7=r9c6]). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r6c7=r9c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c8, r5c8, r4c9, r9c9, [r6c3=r9c2], r6c6} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {col 3, col 6}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 5, box 7}.
      Remainder: {r3c6, r6c3} = {r6c5, r9c2}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Branch on r3c6, r6c9:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
        3.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r6c9], r4c6, r5c6, r6c5}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r6c9). Add edge.
        4.  Guardian: r9c8 guards oddagon {r9c8, r3c8, r3c6, r6c9, r8c9} (source r3c9).
            â†’ color(r9c8) = color(r3c9). Identify.
        5.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c9=r9c8], r6c9, r6c5, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c9=r9c8], r6c5} and {r6c9, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c9=r9c8]) = color(r6c5) = X
              row 3 {r3c6, r3c8, [r3c9=r9c8]}: [r3c9=r9c8]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c8â‰ X

              col 9 {[r3c9=r9c8], r6c9, r8c9}: [r3c9=r9c8]=X â†’ r8c9â‰ X

              [r3c9=r9c8]=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {r3c6, r4c6, r5c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c9) = color(r9c2) = X
              row 6 {r6c3, r6c5, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r6c3, r6c5â‰ X

              col 3 {r6c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X

      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r3c6) = color(r6c3) = X
        row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c6=X â†’ r3c3, r3c9â‰ X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}: r6c3=X â†’ r6c6, r6c9â‰ X

=== Greedy proof (diamond) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r8c8, r8c4, r8c5, r9c5} (spine r8c4â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r8c8) = color(r9c5). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r7c9, [r8c8=r9c5], r9c7, r9c3} (spine [r8c8=r9c5]â€”r9c7).
      â†’ color(r7c9) = color(r9c3). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r3c6, r3c3, r3c9, [r7c9=r9c3]} (spine r3c3â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color([r7c9=r9c3]). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c6=r7c9=r9c3], r6c3, r6c6, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (SET+ladder) ===

  2.  SET: {row 8, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 8, box 9}.
      Remainder: {r9c3} = {r7c9}.
      â†’ color(r9c3) = color(r7c9), but they share a house. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
      row 3: r3c3, r3c6 (also r3c9)
      row 6: r6c3, r6c6 (also r6c8)
      row 8: r8c3, r8c5 (also r8c8)
      row 9: r9c2, r9c6 (also r9c8)
      Rows 8 and 9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7), r8c5â†”r9c6 (box 8), r8c8â†”r9c8 (col 8) â€” derangement â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 3 and 6: r3c3â†”r6c3 (col 3), r3c6â†”r6c6 (col 6) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r3c9 and r6c8).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 3, row 6, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 3, row 6, row 8, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r3â†”r6: r3c3â†”r6c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r8: r3c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r9: r3c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r8: r6c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r6â†”r9: r6c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r3c9) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r6c8], r7c9, r8c8, r9c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      â†’ color(r8c5) = color(r9c9). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, [r8c5=r9c9], r8c9, r8c8} (spine [r8c5=r9c9]â€”r8c9).

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (SET+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  SET: {row 3, row 6, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 3, col 6, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r6c8, r9c5} = {r7c6, r8c9}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c8, r8c8, r8c5, r9c5}:
      Diagonals: {r6c8, r8c5} and {r8c8, r9c5} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r6c8) = color(r8c5) = X
        row 6 {r6c3, r6c6, r6c8}: r6c8=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
        row 8 {r8c5, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c8, r8c9â‰ X

        box 9 {r8c8, r8c9, r9c9}: r8c8, r8c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X
        r9c9=X â†’ r3c9, r9c3â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 3 {r3c3, r6c3, r9c3}: r6c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r3c3=X
        col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6, r7c6â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
        r3c3=X and r3c6=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c8) = color(r9c5) = X
        row 8 {r8c5, r8c8, r8c9}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c5, r8c9â‰ X
        row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c9}: r9c5=X â†’ r9c3, r9c9â‰ X
        col 9 {r3c9, r8c9, r9c9}: r8c9, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X

        r8c8=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        col 3 {r3c3, r6c3, r9c3}: r3c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r6c3=X

        r6c3=X and r6c6=X are adjacent

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (hexagon+xwing+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c3, r8c9:
      Case A: color(r3c3) = color(r8c9). Identify.
        2.  Guardian: r6c8 guards oddagon {r6c8, r6c3, r9c3, r9c7, r8c8} (source [r3c3=r8c9]).
            â†’ color(r6c8) = color([r3c3=r8c9]). Identify.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c6, r7c6, r8c5}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r7c6} and {r6c6, r8c5} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]) = color(r7c6) = X
              row 3 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r3c6, r3c9}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r6c6}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c5, r8c8}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r8c5, r8c8â‰ X
              col 3 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r9c3}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c5}: r7c6=X â†’ r9c5â‰ X
              box 9 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c8, r9c7}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c5, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c6) = color(r8c5) = X
              row 6 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r6c6}: r6c6=X â†’ [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c5, r8c8}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X
              col 3 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r9c3}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3â‰ X â†’ r9c3=X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c5}: r8c5=X â†’ r9c5â‰ X
              box 9 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c8, r9c7}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c8â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
              r9c3=X and r9c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c3) â‰  color(r8c9). Add edge.
        4.  Bridged hexagon: ring {r3c3, r6c3, r6c6, r7c6, r8c5, r8c9}
            Bridges: r9c3â€”r9c5 (edges r3c3â€”r6c3 â†” r7c6â€”r8c5), r6c8â€”r8c8 (edges r6c3â€”r6c6 â†” r8c5â€”r8c9), r3c6â€”r3c9 (edges r6c6â€”r7c6 â†” r8c9â€”r3c3).
            Each bridge forces opposite edges to miss different colors,
            but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

      Case A: color(r3c3) = color(r8c9). Identify.
        2.  Guardian: r6c8 guards oddagon {r6c8, r6c3, r9c3, r9c7, r8c8} (source [r3c3=r8c9]).
            â†’ color(r6c8) = color([r3c3=r8c9]). Identify.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c6, r7c6, r8c5}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r7c6} and {r6c6, r8c5} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]) = color(r7c6) = X
              row 3 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r3c6, r3c9}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 6 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r6c6}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r6c3, r6c6â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c5, r8c8}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r8c5, r8c8â‰ X
              col 3 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r9c3}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r7c6=X â†’ r9c4â‰ X
              box 9 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c8, r9c7}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9]=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c3, r9c4, r9c7}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c6) = color(r8c5) = X
              row 6 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r6c6}: r6c6=X â†’ [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3â‰ X
              row 8 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c5, r8c8}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X
              col 3 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3, r9c3}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r6c3â‰ X â†’ r9c3=X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c4}: r8c5=X â†’ r9c4â‰ X
              box 9 {[r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c8, r9c7}: [r3c3=r6c8=r8c9], r8c8â‰ X â†’ r9c7=X
              r9c3=X and r9c7=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c3) â‰  color(r8c9). Add edge.
        4.  Bridged hexagon: ring {r3c3, r6c3, r6c6, r7c6, r8c5, r8c9}
            Bridges: r9c3â€”r9c4 (edges r3c3â€”r6c3 â†” r7c6â€”r8c5), r6c8â€”r8c8 (edges r6c3â€”r6c6 â†” r8c5â€”r8c9), r3c6â€”r3c9 (edges r6c6â€”r7c6 â†” r8c9â€”r3c3).
            Each bridge forces opposite edges to miss different colors,
            but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r7c9, r9c9, r8c8} (spine r7c9â€”r9c9).

=== Greedy proof (parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
      row 3: r3c3, r3c6 (also r3c9)
      row 7: r7c3, r7c6 (also r7c8)
      row 8: r8c3, r8c5
      row 9: r9c2, r9c6
      Rows 7, 8, 9: {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} in box 7, {r7c6, r8c5, r9c6} in box 8 â€” circular ladder of length 3 â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 3 and 7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3), r3c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r3c9 and r7c8).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r3â†”r7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r8: r3c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r9: r3c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r7â†”r8: r7c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r7â†”r9: r7c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
          r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r3c9) = color(r7c8). Identify.
  2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r7c8], r5c9, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (diamond+parity) ===

          r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.

      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color([r3c3=r9c2]) = color([r3c3=r9c2]). Identify.

              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r9c6â‰ X
              col 9 {[r3c9=r8c8], r5c9, r7c9}: [r3c9=r8c8]=X â†’ r5c9, r7c9â‰ X
              box 9 {[r3c9=r8c8], r7c9, r9c7}: [r3c9=r8c8]=X â†’ r9c7â‰ X
              [r3c9=r8c8]=X â†’ r6c8, r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c6, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r3c3, r8c3â‰ X â†’ r7c3=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3=X and r9c2=X clash
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c9) â‰  color(r8c8). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r7c3, r7c9, r8c8, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {r7c3, r8c8} and {r7c9, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r7c3) = color(r8c8) = X
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r7c3=X â†’ r3c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c7}: r8c8=X â†’ r7c9, r9c7â‰ X
              r8c8=X â†’ r3c9, r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c2, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6=X and r9c6=X clash
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c9) = color(r8c3) = X
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r3c3, r7c3â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r7c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
              box 9 {r7c9, r8c8, r9c7}: r7c9=X â†’ r8c8, r9c7â‰ X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c6, r9c7}: r9c2, r9c7â‰ X â†’ r9c6=X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r9c6}: r3c6=X and r9c6=X clash
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r3c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, [r3c3=r9c2], r3c6, r9c6} (spine [r3c3=r9c2]â€”r3c6).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {[r3c9=r9c6], r7c9, r9c7, r8c8} (spine r7c9â€”r9c7).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r9c6]) = color(r8c8). Identify.
  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c9=r8c8=r9c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {[r3c3=r9c2], r3c6, r6c6, r6c8, r5c9, r7c9, r9c7} (length 7) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_guardian] (diamond+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} (spine r7c3â€”r8c3).
      â†’ color(r3c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c9, [r3c3=r9c2], r3c6, r9c6} (spine [r3c3=r9c2]â€”r3c6).
      â†’ color(r3c9) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r5c9, [r3c9=r9c6], r7c9, r9c7} (spine [r3c9=r9c6]â€”r7c9).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r9c7). Identify.
  4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r9c7], r6c8, r6c6}:
      Diagonals: {[r3c9=r9c6], r6c8} and {[r5c9=r9c7], r6c6} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r3c9=r9c6]) = color(r6c8) = X
        row 3 {[r3c3=r9c2], r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6]}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ [r3c3=r9c2], r3c6â‰ X
        row 9 {[r3c3=r9c2], [r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r9c7]}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ [r5c9=r9c7]â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6], r6c6}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r9c7], r7c9}: [r3c9=r9c6]=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
        box 9 {[r5c9=r9c7], r7c9, r8c8}: [r5c9=r9c7], r7c9â‰ X â†’ r8c8=X
        r8c8=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
        r6c8=X and r8c8=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color([r5c9=r9c7]) = color(r6c6) = X
        row 9 {[r3c3=r9c2], [r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r9c7]}: [r5c9=r9c7]=X â†’ [r3c3=r9c2], [r3c9=r9c6]â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6], r6c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
        col 9 {[r3c9=r9c6], [r5c9=r9c7], r7c9}: [r5c9=r9c7]=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X
        box 9 {[r5c9=r9c7], r7c9, r8c8}: [r5c9=r9c7]=X â†’ r8c8â‰ X
        [r5c9=r9c7]=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 3 {[r3c3=r9c2], r3c6, [r3c9=r9c6]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c3=r5c9=r6c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, [r7c9=r9c2], r8c3, r8c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  Diamond {r3c6, r3c9, [r5c9=r6c6], r7c9} (spine r3c9â€”[r5c9=r6c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r7c9). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c3, [r3c6=r7c9], r8c6, r8c3}:
      Diagonals: {r3c3, r8c6} and {[r3c6=r7c9], r8c3} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r3c3) = color(r8c6) = X
        row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r7c9], r3c9}: r3c3=X â†’ [r3c6=r7c9], r3c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r3c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r3c3=X â†’ r8c3, r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {[r3c6=r7c9], [r5c9=r6c6], r8c6}: r8c6=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c6]â‰ X
        col 9 {[r3c6=r7c9], r3c9, [r5c9=r6c6]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color([r3c6=r7c9]) = color(r8c3) = X
        row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r7c9], r3c9}: [r3c6=r7c9]=X â†’ r3c3, r3c9â‰ X
        col 3 {r3c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c3â‰ X
        col 6 {[r3c6=r7c9], [r5c9=r6c6], r8c6}: [r3c6=r7c9]=X â†’ [r5c9=r6c6], r8c6â‰ X
        box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
        [r3c6=r7c9]=X â†’ r9c8â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).
      â†’ color(r5c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
  2.  SET: {row 3, row 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{col 3, col 9}.
      Remainder: {r3c6, r9c2, r9c8} = {[r5c9=r6c6], r7c9, r8c3}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r7c9, r8c3, r8c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+oddwheel+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r7c9, r8c6:
      Case A: color(r7c9) = color(r8c6). Identify.
        2.  Guardian: r6c7 guards oddagon {r6c7, r5c9, r3c9, r3c6, r6c6} (source [r7c9=r8c6]).
            â†’ color(r6c7) = color([r7c9=r8c6]). Identify.
        3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c7=r7c9=r8c6] forces rim to 2 colors.
            Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, r6c8, r6c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r7c9) â‰  color(r8c6). Add edge.
        4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c3, r3c6, r8c6, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {r3c3, r8c6} and {r3c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c3) = color(r8c6) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r8c6}: r8c6=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X
              r3c3=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c6=X â†’ r7c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r3c9, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
              box 6 {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8}: r5c9=X â†’ r6c7, r6c8â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c6, r6c7, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c6=X â†’ r3c3, r3c9â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r8c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r6c6, r8c6â‰ X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c8}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
              r9c8=X â†’ r6c8, r7c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 6 {r6c6, r6c7, r6c8}: r6c6, r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c7=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r7c9}: r3c9, r7c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
              box 6 {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8}: r5c9=X and r6c7=X clash
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET+oddwheel) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).

      Remainder: {r3c9, r9c2, r9c5} = {[r5c9=r6c6], r7c6, r8c3}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r8c9, r8c3, r7c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_guardian] (diamond+xwing) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8, r6c6} (spine r6c7â€”r6c8).

        row 9 {[r6c5=r9c6], r9c2, r9c3}: [r6c5=r9c6]=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
        col 3 {r3c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r3c3â‰ X
        col 5 {[r6c5=r9c6], r7c5, r8c5}: [r6c5=r9c6]=X â†’ r7c5, r8c5â‰ X
        box 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], r5c9, r6c8}: [r3c6=r6c9], r6c8â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        r5c9=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r6c9], r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c5) = color(r9c2) = X
        row 9 {[r6c5=r9c6], r9c2, r9c3}: r9c2=X â†’ [r6c5=r9c6], r9c3â‰ X
        col 5 {[r6c5=r9c6], r7c5, r8c5}: r8c5=X â†’ r7c5â‰ X

        row 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], [r6c5=r9c6], r6c8}: [r3c6=r6c9], [r6c5=r9c6]â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
        col 9 {[r3c6=r6c9], r3c9, r5c9}: [r3c6=r6c9], r3c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        box 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], r5c9, r6c8}: r5c9=X and r6c8=X clash
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET+oddwheel) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c3, r8c3, r9c3, r9c2} (spine r8c3â€”r9c3).
      â†’ color(r3c3) = color(r9c2). Identify.
  2.  SET: {col 3, col 5, col 9}â€‰âˆ’â€‰{box 6, box 7, box 8}.
      Remainder: {[r3c3=r9c2], r3c9, r6c5} = {[r3c3=r9c2], r6c8, r9c6}.
      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.
  3.  Odd wheel: hub [r3c3=r9c2] forces rim to 2 colors.
      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c5, r6c8, r9c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              box 8 {[r3c3=r7c5], r8c5, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r8c5â‰ X
              r9c6=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {[r3c3=r7c5], r3c6, r3c9}: [r3c3=r7c5], r3c6â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              col 5 {[r3c3=r7c5], r6c5, r8c5}: [r3c3=r7c5], r8c5â‰ X â†’ r6c5=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r5c9, r6c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r5c9, r6c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c9, r6c8, r6c9}: r5c9, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
              r6c5=X and r6c8=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c5) = color(r9c2) = X
              col 5 {[r3c3=r7c5], r6c5, r8c5}: r8c5=X â†’ [r3c3=r7c5], r6c5â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X

              col 3 {[r3c3=r7c5], r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c3) â‰  color(r7c5). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r7c3, r7c5, r9c6, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {r7c3, r9c6} and {r7c5, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r7c3) = color(r9c6) = X
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r7c3=X â†’ r3c3, r8c3â‰ X

              r9c6=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3, r3c6â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              col 5 {r6c5, r7c5, r8c5}: r7c5, r8c5â‰ X â†’ r6c5=X

              r6c5=X and r6c8=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 2/3 [no_set] (diamond+xwing+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r6c5, r7c5, r8c5, r9c6} (spine r7c5â€”r8c5).
      â†’ color(r6c5) = color(r9c6). Identify.
  2.  Guardian: r3c6 guards oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r5c9, r6c8, [r6c5=r9c6]} (source r6c9).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r6c9). Identify.
  3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r6c5=r9c6], r7c5, r7c3, r9c2}:
      Diagonals: {[r6c5=r9c6], r7c3} and {r7c5, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color([r6c5=r9c6]) = color(r7c3) = X
        row 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], [r6c5=r9c6], r6c8}: [r6c5=r9c6]=X â†’ [r3c6=r6c9], r6c8â‰ X
        col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r7c3=X â†’ r3c3, r8c3â‰ X
        col 5 {[r6c5=r9c6], r7c5, r8c5}: [r6c5=r9c6]=X â†’ r7c5, r8c5â‰ X
        box 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], r5c9, r6c8}: [r3c6=r6c9], r6c8â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
        r5c9=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r6c9], r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r7c5) = color(r9c2) = X
        col 5 {[r6c5=r9c6], r7c5, r8c5}: r7c5=X â†’ [r6c5=r9c6], r8c5â‰ X
        box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
        col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r7c3, r8c3â‰ X â†’ r3c3=X
        r3c3=X â†’ [r3c6=r6c9], r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        row 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], [r6c5=r9c6], r6c8}: [r3c6=r6c9], [r6c5=r9c6]â‰ X â†’ r6c8=X
        col 9 {[r3c6=r6c9], r3c9, r5c9}: [r3c6=r6c9], r3c9â‰ X â†’ r5c9=X
        box 6 {[r3c6=r6c9], r5c9, r6c8}: r5c9=X and r6c8=X clash
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+SET+oddwheel) ===

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

      Bivalue oddagon {r3c6, r3c9, r6c5, r6c8, r9c6} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r7c3:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r7c3). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r7c3], r8c3, r8c5, r9c6}:

            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c6=r7c3]) = color(r8c5) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r7c3], r3c9}: [r3c6=r7c3]=X â†’ r3c3, r3c9â‰ X

              row 9 {r9c2, r9c5, r9c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c3) = color(r9c6) = X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c5, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r9c2, r9c5â‰ X
              col 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r7c3], r8c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r3c3, [r3c6=r7c3]â‰ X
              box 8 {r8c5, r9c5, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r8c5â‰ X
              row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r7c3], r3c9}: r3c3, [r3c6=r7c3]â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X

              r3c9=X â†’ r5c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              r6c5=X â†’ r6c7, r6c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 6 {r5c9, r6c7, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r7c3). Add edge.

            Diagonals: {r3c6, r9c2} and {r7c3, r9c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

              r8c5=X â†’ r6c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c3) = color(r9c6) = X

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/3 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c3, r6c9:
      Case A: color(r3c3) = color(r6c9). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c3=r6c9], r3c6, r8c6, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c3=r6c9], r8c6} and {r3c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c3=r6c9]) = color(r8c6) = X
              row 3 {[r3c3=r6c9], r3c6, r3c9}: [r3c3=r6c9]=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c6=X â†’ r8c3, r8c8â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r7c6, r8c6}: r8c6=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
              col 9 {[r3c3=r6c9], r3c9, r9c9}: [r3c3=r6c9]=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X
              box 6 {[r3c3=r6c9], r5c8, r6c8}: [r3c3=r6c9]=X â†’ r5c8, r6c8â‰ X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c6, r9c5}: r8c6=X â†’ r9c5â‰ X

              col 8 {r5c8, r6c8, r8c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r3c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 3 {[r3c3=r6c9], r3c6, r3c9}: r3c6=X â†’ [r3c3=r6c9], r3c9â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r7c6, r8c6}: r3c6=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X
              col 9 {[r3c3=r6c9], r3c9, r9c9}: [r3c3=r6c9], r3c9â‰ X â†’ r9c9=X

              r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c5=X and r9c9=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c3) â‰  color(r6c9). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c8, r9c9, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c9} and {r8c8, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

              Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c6, r9c2:
      Case A: color(r3c6) = color(r9c2). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r3c6=r9c2], r7c6, r8c4, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {[r3c6=r9c2], r8c4} and {r7c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r3c6=r9c2]) = color(r8c4) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r9c2], r3c9}: [r3c6=r9c2]=X â†’ r3c3, r3c9â‰ X

              col 8 {r5c8, r6c8, r9c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c4, r8c5}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c4, r8c5â‰ X
              r7c6=X â†’ [r3c6=r9c2]â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c3=X â†’ r3c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, [r3c6=r9c2], r3c9}: r3c3, [r3c6=r9c2]â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r6c9, r9c9â‰ X
              row 9 {[r3c6=r9c2], r9c8, r9c9}: [r3c6=r9c2], r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
              col 8 {r5c8, r6c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r5c8, r6c8â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r6c8, r6c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r9c2). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c3, r3c6, r9c2, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {r3c3, r9c2} and {r3c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

  1.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c5, r9c6, r9c2}:
      Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c6} and {r8c5, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
      Case 1: color(r8c3) = color(r9c6) = X
        col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r3c3, r7c3â‰ X
        col 6 {r3c6, r7c6, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r3c6, r7c6â‰ X
        box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c6}: r9c6=X â†’ r8c5â‰ X
        row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3, r3c6â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
        row 7 {r7c3, r7c6, r7c8}: r7c3, r7c6â‰ X â†’ r7c8=X
        col 8 {r5c8, r6c8, r7c8}: r7c8=X â†’ r5c8, r6c8â‰ X
        box 6 {r5c8, r6c8, r6c9}: r5c8, r6c8â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X
        r3c9=X and r6c9=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.
      Case 2: color(r8c5) = color(r9c2) = X
        box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r9c2=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
        box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r9c6}: r8c5=X â†’ r7c6, r9c6â‰ X

        r3c3=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
        r3c3=X and r3c6=X are adjacent
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Greedy proof (parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
      row 3: r3c3, r3c6 (also r3c9)
      row 7: r7c3, r7c6 (also r7c8)
      row 8: r8c3, r8c5
      row 9: r9c2, r9c6
      Rows 7, 8, 9: {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} in box 7, {r7c6, r8c5, r9c6} in box 8 â€” circular ladder of length 3 â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 3 and 7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3), r3c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r3c9 and r7c8).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r3â†”r7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r8: r3c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r9: r3c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.
          r7â†”r8: r7c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r7â†”r9: r7c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
          r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r3c9) = color(r7c8). Identify.
  2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r7c8], r5c8, r6c8, r6c9}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+parity) ===

=== Alt proof 2/2 [no_xwing] (SET+hexagon) ===

      â†’ color(r9c2) = color(r5c8). Identify.

      Bridges: r3c3â€”r9c3 (edges r3c6â€”r3c9 â†” [r5c8=r9c2]â€”r8c3), r9c9â€”r8c8 (edges r3c9â€”r6c9 â†” r8c3â€”r8c6), r6c8â€”r6c6 (edges r6c9â€”[r5c8=r9c2] â†” r8c6â€”r3c6).

      but every 3-coloring of Câ‚† requires at least one matching pair. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r6c6, r9c9:
      Case A: color(r6c6) = color(r9c9). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c8, r6c7, [r6c6=r9c9], r7c8}:
            Diagonals: {r5c8, [r6c6=r9c9]} and {r6c7, r7c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r5c8) = color([r6c6=r9c9]) = X
              row 6 {[r6c6=r9c9], r6c7, r6c9}: [r6c6=r9c9]=X â†’ r6c7, r6c9â‰ X
              row 9 {[r6c6=r9c9], r9c1, r9c2}: [r6c6=r9c9]=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, [r6c6=r9c9], r7c6}: [r6c6=r9c9]=X â†’ r3c6, r7c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, [r6c6=r9c9], r6c9}: [r6c6=r9c9]=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              r5c8=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c3=X â†’ r3c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r6c7) = color(r7c8) = X
              row 6 {[r6c6=r9c9], r6c7, r6c9}: r6c7=X â†’ [r6c6=r9c9], r6c9â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, [r6c6=r9c9], r6c9}: [r6c6=r9c9], r6c9â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              box 6 {r5c8, r6c7, r6c9}: r6c7=X â†’ r5c8â‰ X
              r3c9=X â†’ r3c3, r3c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              r7c8=X â†’ r7c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {r3c6, [r6c6=r9c9], r7c6}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r6c6) â‰  color(r9c9). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r6c6, r7c6, r7c8, r9c9}:
            Diagonals: {r6c6, r7c8} and {r7c6, r9c9} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r6c6) = color(r7c8) = X
              row 6 {r6c6, r6c7, r6c9}: r6c6=X â†’ r6c7, r6c9â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6, r7c6â‰ X
              box 6 {r5c8, r6c7, r6c9}: r6c7, r6c9â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X
              r6c6=X â†’ r9c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              r5c8=X and r7c8=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c6) = color(r9c9) = X
              row 9 {r9c1, r9c2, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r9c1, r9c2â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r7c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r3c6, r6c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r6c9, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r3c9, r6c9â‰ X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c1, r9c2}: r9c1, r9c2â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              r7c6=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c3=X â†’ r3c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  2.  Diamond {r6c8, r6c6, [r6c7=r7c8], r7c6} (spine r6c6â€”[r6c7=r7c8]).

  5.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c3=r9c9], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch+guardian) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Guardian: r7c6 guards oddagon {r7c6, r6c6, r6c7, r5c8, r7c8} (source r6c8).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r6c8). Identify.
  2.  Branch on r3c9, r8c3:
      Case A: color(r3c9) = color(r8c3). Identify.
        3.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r8c3], r9c2, r9c3, r9c9}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c9) â‰  color(r8c3). Add edge.
        4.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c9, r8c3, r9c2, r9c9}:
            Diagonals: {r3c9, r9c2} and {r8c3, r9c9} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c9) = color(r9c2) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c3, r3c6â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c9}: r9c2=X â†’ r9c3, r9c9â‰ X
              col 3 {r3c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r3c3, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r8c3=X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X and r9c2=X clash
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c3) = color(r9c9) = X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c9}: r9c9=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
              col 3 {r3c3, r8c3, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r3c3â‰ X
              r8c3=X â†’ r3c9â‰ X (adjacent)
              r9c9=X â†’ r7c8â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c6=X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, [r6c8=r7c6]}: r3c6=X â†’ r6c6, [r6c8=r7c6]â‰ X
              col 8 {r5c8, [r6c8=r7c6], r7c8}: [r6c8=r7c6], r7c8â‰ X â†’ r5c8=X
              box 6 {r5c8, r6c7, [r6c8=r7c6]}: r5c8=X â†’ r6c7â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c6, r6c7, [r6c8=r7c6]}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+oddwheel+branch+guardian) ===

  1.  Guardian: r7c6 guards oddagon {r7c6, r6c6, r6c7, r5c8, r7c8} (source r6c8).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r6c8). Identify.

        3.  Odd wheel: hub [r6c8=r7c6] forces rim to 2 colors.

              r3c3=X and r8c3=X are adjacent
              Contradiction.

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_set] (diamond+xwing+guardian) ===

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9, r6c8} (spine r4c9â€”r5c9).

      By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.

=== Greedy proof (parity) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
      row 3: r3c3, r3c6 (also r3c9)
      row 7: r7c3, r7c6 (also r7c8)

      Rows 7, 8, 9: {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2} in box 7, {r7c6, r8c5, r9c6} in box 8 â€” circular ladder of length 3 â†’ same Î´.
      Rows 3 and 7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3), r3c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r3c9 and r7c8).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.
        row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 pairwise distinct:
          r3â†”r7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r8: r3c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r9: r3c6â†”r9c6 (col 6) â†’ pairs differ.

          r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r3c9) = color(r7c8). Identify.
  2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r7c8], r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  4.  Diamond {r3c6, [r3c3=r6c6], [r3c9=r6c8=r8c6], r8c3} (spine [r3c3=r6c6]â€”[r3c9=r6c8=r8c6]).
      â†’ color(r3c6) = color(r8c3). Identify.
  5.  Diamond {[r3c9=r6c8=r8c6], [r3c3=r6c6], [r3c6=r8c3], r7c3} (spine [r3c3=r6c6]â€”[r3c6=r8c3]).
      â†’ color([r3c9=r6c8=r8c6]) = color(r7c3). Identify.
  6.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r6c8=r7c3=r8c6], r9c2, r9c4, r9c5}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c9, r6c6:
      Case A: color(r3c9) = color(r6c6). Identify.
        2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r6c6], r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c9) â‰  color(r6c6). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c9, r4c9, r6c8, r6c6}:
            Diagonals: {r3c9, r6c8} and {r4c9, r6c6} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c9) = color(r6c8) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r3c3, r3c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r4c9, r5c9â‰ X
              r3c9=X â†’ r6c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r8c6}: r3c6, r6c6â‰ X â†’ r8c6=X
              box 8 {r8c6, r9c4, r9c5}: r8c6=X â†’ r9c4, r9c5â‰ X
              r8c6=X â†’ r8c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c4, r9c5}: r9c4, r9c5â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r3c3, r8c3â‰ X â†’ r7c3=X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3=X and r9c2=X clash
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r4c9) = color(r6c6) = X
              col 6 {r3c6, r6c6, r8c6}: r6c6=X â†’ r3c6, r8c6â‰ X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r5c9}: r4c9=X â†’ r3c9, r5c9â‰ X
              box 6 {r4c9, r5c9, r6c8}: r4c9=X â†’ r6c8â‰ X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c6, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r3c3=X
              col 3 {r3c3, r7c3, r8c3}: r3c3=X â†’ r7c3, r8c3â‰ X
              box 7 {r7c3, r8c3, r9c2}: r7c3, r8c3â‰ X â†’ r9c2=X
              r9c2=X â†’ r9c4, r9c5â‰ X (adjacent)
              box 8 {r8c6, r9c4, r9c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

        col 5 {r6c5, r7c5, r8c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
        Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/2 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c3, r7c5:
      Case A: color(r3c3) = color(r7c5). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r8c3, r8c5, r9c6, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {r8c3, r9c6} and {r8c5, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r8c3) = color(r9c6) = X

              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c3) â‰  color(r7c5). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r7c3, r7c5, r9c6, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {r7c3, r9c6} and {r7c5, r9c2} (non-adjacent).

      â†’ Both sides forced all-distinct. Virtual edges added.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  1.  Diamond {r7c6, r9c4, r9c5, r9c2} (spine r9c4â€”r9c5).
      â†’ color(r7c6) = color(r9c2). Identify.

=== Alt proof 3/3 [no_xwing] (diamond+oddwheel) ===

      Bivalue oddagon {[r3c6=r9c5], r3c9, [r4c9=r8c8], r8c3, r9c2} (length 5) cannot be 2-colored. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r3c3, r7c6:
      Case A: color(r3c3) = color(r7c6). Identify.
        2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c3=r7c6], r8c3, r8c5, r8c6}. Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c3) â‰  color(r7c6). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c3, r7c6, r8c5, r8c3}:
            Diagonals: {r3c3, r8c5} and {r7c6, r8c3} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r3c3) = color(r8c5) = X
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3=X â†’ r3c6, r3c9â‰ X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c6}: r8c5=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r7c6, r8c6}: r3c6, r8c6â‰ X â†’ r7c6=X
              box 8 {r7c6, r8c5, r8c6}: r7c6=X and r8c5=X clash
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r7c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c5, r8c6}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c5, r8c6â‰ X
              col 6 {r3c6, r7c6, r8c6}: r7c6=X â†’ r3c6â‰ X
              r7c6=X â†’ r3c3â‰ X (adjacent)
              r8c3=X â†’ r9c2â‰ X (adjacent)
              row 3 {r3c3, r3c6, r3c9}: r3c3, r3c6â‰ X â†’ r3c9=X
              col 9 {r3c9, r4c9, r9c9}: r3c9=X â†’ r4c9, r9c9â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c8, r9c9}: r9c2, r9c9â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X

              box 6 {r4c9, r5c8, r6c8}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Both cases contradict 3-colorability.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r3c6) â‰  color(r9c2). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r3c3, r3c6, r9c2, r8c3}:

=== Greedy proof (parity) ===

  1.  Gradient chain (with identification):
      For each row, Î´ = color(B) âˆ’ color(A) mod 3, where A and B are the first and second listed cells.
      Î´ âˆˆ {âˆ’1, +1}; 3 color pairs per Î´ value.

      Rows 3 and 7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3), r3c6â†”r7c6 (col 6) â€” 2 of 3 positions.
        Relative permutation is 3-cycle (same Î´) or transposition (opposite Î´, fixing r3c9 and r7c8).
      Case 1 (same Î´): row 3, row 7, row 8, row 9 have same Î´. 4 rows; only 3 color pairs per Î´ value.

          r3â†”r7: r3c3â†”r7c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.
          r3â†”r8: r3c3â†”r8c3 (col 3) â†’ pairs differ.

          r7â†”r9: r7c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
          r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
        4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.
      Case 2 (opposite Î´): color(r3c9) = color(r7c8). Identify.
  2.  Kâ‚„ on {[r3c9=r7c8], r4c9, r5c8, r6c8}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_xwing] (diamond+parity) ===

        r8â†”r9: r8c3â†”r9c2 (box 7) â†’ pairs differ.
      4 distinct pairs, only 3 per Î´ class. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

  4.  Kâ‚„ on {[r6c5=r8c8=r9c6], r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r2c9, r5c6:
      Case A: color(r2c9) = color(r5c6). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r2c9=r5c6], r3c8, r6c8, r6c5}:

            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r2c9=r5c6]) = color(r6c8) = X

            Case 2: color(r3c8) = color(r6c5) = X

  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Diamond {r3c8, r2c9, r3c9, r6c9} (spine r2c9â€”r3c9).
      â†’ color(r3c8) = color(r6c9). Identify.

      â†’ color([r3c8=r6c9]) = color(r5c6). Identify.
  3.  Diamond {r8c6, [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9], r8c8, r9c8} (spine [r3c8=r5c6=r6c9]â€”r8c8).
      â†’ color(r8c6) = color(r9c8). Identify.
  4.  Kâ‚„ on {r8c3, [r8c6=r9c8], r9c2, r9c3}. Contradiction.

  Therefore the pattern is not 3-colorable. â–¡

=== Alt proof 1/1 [no_diamond] (xwing+branch) ===
Proof of non-3-colorability:
  Assume for contradiction it is 3-colorable.

  1.  Branch on r5c6, r9c2:
      Case A: color(r5c6) = color(r9c2). Identify.
        2.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {[r5c6=r9c2], r8c6, r8c8, r9c8}:
            Diagonals: {[r5c6=r9c2], r8c8} and {r8c6, r9c8} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color([r5c6=r9c2]) = color(r8c8) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r8c3, r8c6â‰ X
              row 9 {[r5c6=r9c2], r9c3, r9c8}: [r5c6=r9c2]=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X
              box 5 {[r5c6=r9c2], r6c4, r6c5}: [r5c6=r9c2]=X â†’ r6c4, r6c5â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c4, r6c5, r6c9}: r6c4, r6c5â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X
              col 9 {r2c9, r3c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r2c9, r3c9â‰ X
              box 3 {r2c9, r3c8, r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c6) = color(r9c8) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c6=X â†’ r8c3, r8c8â‰ X
              row 9 {[r5c6=r9c2], r9c3, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ [r5c6=r9c2], r9c3â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X
              box 7 {[r5c6=r9c2], r8c3, r9c3}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
      Case B: color(r5c6) â‰  color(r9c2). Add edge.
        3.  Pigeonhole X-wing on {r5c6, r8c6, r8c3, r9c2}:
            Diagonals: {r5c6, r8c3} and {r8c6, r9c2} (non-adjacent).
            By pigeonhole, one diagonal must share a color.
            Case 1: color(r5c6) = color(r8c3) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c3=X â†’ r8c6, r8c8â‰ X
              box 5 {r5c6, r6c4, r6c5}: r5c6=X â†’ r6c4, r6c5â‰ X
              box 7 {r8c3, r9c2, r9c3}: r8c3=X â†’ r9c2, r9c3â‰ X
              row 6 {r6c4, r6c5, r6c9}: r6c4, r6c5â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c8}: r9c2, r9c3â‰ X â†’ r9c8=X
              col 8 {r3c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r9c8=X â†’ r3c8â‰ X
              col 9 {r2c9, r3c9, r6c9}: r6c9=X â†’ r2c9, r3c9â‰ X
              box 3 {r2c9, r3c8, r3c9}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
            Case 2: color(r8c6) = color(r9c2) = X
              row 8 {r8c3, r8c6, r8c8}: r8c6=X â†’ r8c3, r8c8â‰ X
              row 9 {r9c2, r9c3, r9c8}: r9c2=X â†’ r9c3, r9c8â‰ X
              col 8 {r3c8, r8c8, r9c8}: r8c8, r9c8â‰ X â†’ r3c8=X
              box 3 {r2c9, r3c8, r3c9}: r3c8=X â†’ r2c9, r3c9â‰ X
              r8c6=X â†’ r5c6â‰ X (adjacent)
              col 9 {r2c9, r3c9, r6c9}: r2c9, r3c9â‰ X â†’ r6c9=X

              box 5 {r5c6, r6c4, r6c5}: all â‰ X, but one must be X
              Contradiction.
